Check OEM or equivalent — same for 2.5 TDi and 2.8 TDi variants
Do not mix brands — replace all four as a set for even heat distribution

The 4M40 diesel uses four glow plugs for cold starting — they pre-heat the combustion chambers so that compressed air is hot enough to ignite diesel fuel without a spark. A failed glow plug causes difficult cold starting, white smoke on cold start that takes longer than usual to clear, and rough running until the engine warms up. Glow plugs are consumable items and should be inspected or replaced at 80,000–100,000 km intervals or whenever cold starting becomes difficult.

## Symptoms

- Hard starting in cold weather (engine cranks but is reluctant to fire)
- White smoke on cold start that takes more than 2 minutes to clear
- Rough misfiring idle when engine is cold that smooths out when warm
- Glow plug warning light stays on longer than usual before cycling off
- One cylinder consistently misfires on cold start (single failed plug)

## Tools needed

- Glow plug socket (typically 10 mm hex)
- Extension bar and ratchet
- Torque wrench
- Penetrating oil (WD-40 or equivalent)
- Multimeter (for testing before removing)
- Anti-seize compound (for installation)

## Tips

- Use anti-seize on the new plug threads to ensure the next set can be removed without risk of breakage.
- Glow plug relay failure (not the plugs themselves) is also common and produces identical symptoms. Test the relay before replacing all plugs.
- On the 4M40, the glow plug relay is typically in the engine bay fuse/relay box. It can be tested by swapping with an identical relay.
- If the engine starts well in warm weather but is hard to start only in winter, the plugs are likely marginal — not completely failed. Replace before winter arrives.
4M40 Diesel — Injection Pump Top Seal and Governor Cover Repair
Contains all O-rings and seals for the pump. You will use items 4, 6, and the timer cover O-rings.
Item 6 from kit — the main top-plate seal. New design has moulded nibs to prevent rolling.
Timer cover O-ring (cold start advance / CSD cover)
Item 1 from kit — same O-ring used three times

The Zexel (formerly Diesel Kiki) injection pump fitted to the 4M40 diesel (2.5 TDi and 2.8 TDi) has a known weakness: the governor cover top-plate seal rolls over under continuous fuel pressure, allowing fuel to seep from the top of the pump. The replacement part (updated design with moulded nibs to prevent rolling) is available in the Zexel repair kit and can be replaced with the pump on the engine — no pump removal or re-calibration is required. This procedure also covers the throttle shaft O-ring, which is the other common leak point. Do NOT confuse this with the front shaft seal (which does require pump removal and diesel-specialist recalibration).

## Symptoms

- Fuel odour from the engine bay, especially after driving
- Visible fuel staining or wet area on top of the injection pump
- Fuel seeping around the governor cover (top plate) or throttle shaft
- Fuel consumption higher than usual in severe cases

## Tools needed

- 5 mm allen key / hex socket (for top cover cap screws)
- 10 mm combination spanner (throttle arm nut)
- 17 mm combination spanner (optional, useful)
- 6-inch thin steel rule (for guiding linkage during reassembly)
- Needle-nose pliers
- Two small flat-blade screwdrivers
- Felt-tip marker or Tipp-Ex correction fluid (for alignment marks)
- Piece of string (reassembly aid — no knots)
- Degreaser and hose-down water
- Rag and drain tray

## Tips

- The string trick for reassembly is the hardest part — take time to get this right. A dropped string inside the pump means disassembly again.
- If you damaged the governor cover seal groove with a screwdriver during removal, the new seal may not seal correctly. Clean up with a fine file if needed.
- Do not disturb the idle speed or maximum fuel stop screws unless necessary — they are factory calibrated and difficult to set correctly by eye.
- The front shaft seal (where the pump drive shaft enters) requires pump removal and bench work by a diesel specialist. Do not attempt it in situ.
- After the repair, run the engine and check thoroughly for any remaining leaks. If the top is now dry but the front is wet, you have a front seal leak — different job.
- Zexel kit 146600-1120 is widely available from diesel injection specialists. A complete kit is worth having even if you only use a few parts.

4M41 Diesel — Carbon Deposit Removal (EGR, Inlet Manifold, Injectors)
Replace whenever EGR valve is removed
If coolant is mixing with EGR gases — white smoke from exhaust and coolant consumption
Diesel intake cleaner spray (Wynn's, Forte, etc.)
Aerosol cleaner for use with engine running to clean valves and manifold

The 4M41 diesel, like most modern direct-injection diesels with EGR systems, builds up carbon deposits in the inlet manifold, EGR cooler, and around the inlet valves over time. This restricts airflow, reduces power, worsens fuel economy, and can cause rough idle or black smoke. The 4M41 common-rail Gen 4 version is particularly prone due to the EGR system recirculating exhaust gases back into the inlet. Regular cleaning at high mileage (or when symptoms appear) restores performance. The EGR valve itself can also stick open or closed from carbon build-up, triggering fault codes.

## Symptoms

- Loss of power, especially above 2,500 rpm
- Black smoke from exhaust under load
- Rough idle with slight smoke
- Increased fuel consumption
- EGR-related fault codes (P0400, P0401, P0402)
- MAF / air flow sensor codes following carbon build-up restricting intake

## Tools needed

- Socket set and screwdrivers
- Wire brush (NOT metallic on aluminium parts)
- Plastic scraper
- Rags and cleaning cloths
- Torque wrench
- EGR valve cleaner spray or carburettor cleaner

## Tips

- After any intake cleaning, clear EGR fault codes and test-drive. If codes return, the EGR valve itself may be mechanically faulty (internal fault, not just carbon).
- On Gen 4 (common rail) models, excessive carbon is often caused by a malfunctioning EGR that stays open too long. Cleaning helps temporarily, but diagnosing the root cause is important.
- Blanking the EGR (blocking it off) is a common modification in markets where it is legal — it prevents carbon re-entry entirely. Check local emissions regulations before doing this.
- Do NOT spray large quantities of aerosol cleaner into the intake at once — this can cause hydraulic lock (liquid in cylinder) and bent connecting rods.
- The EGR cooler (a heat exchanger that cools exhaust gas before re-entry) can crack over time, mixing coolant with EGR gases. Signs: white steam from exhaust and unexplained coolant loss. Requires EGR cooler replacement.

The 4M41 diesel uses a timing chain rather than a belt, so there is no scheduled replacement interval. However, the chain tensioner is a wear item — a failing tensioner causes chain rattle on start-up (especially cold) and, left untreated, can allow the chain to jump timing with catastrophic results. Chain rattle that clears after 30 seconds of idle is the classic symptom. Replacement is a significant job requiring front engine access but is straightforward for a competent home mechanic.

## Symptoms

- Rattling noise from the front of the engine on cold start, clearing within 10–30 seconds
- Rattling that gets louder over time or takes longer to clear
- Timing chain noise at idle that does not clear (advanced wear)
- Check engine light with camshaft/crankshaft correlation codes

## Tools needed

- Socket set and ratchet
- Torque wrench
- Drain tray for coolant / oil
- RTV sealant if required by gasket type

## Tips

- If you hear chain rattle only at cold start that clears quickly, the tensioner is losing oil pressure overnight. Replace soon but it is not an immediate emergency.
- Continuous or worsening rattle requires prompt attention — a jumped chain can bend valves on the 4M41.
- While the front cover is off, inspect the chain guide rails for wear and replace if the plastic surface is worn through to metal.
- Change the engine oil and filter after this job — metal debris from a worn chain or guides may be in the oil.

4M41 Diesel — Valve Clearance Check and Adjustment
Reuse if in good condition, but have one on hand
Replace if hardened or cracked when removed
Optional but recommended when glow plugs are out

Valve clearance (also called valve lash) on the 4M41 diesel must be checked and adjusted with the engine cold. Correct clearance is critical for engine longevity and performance — too tight causes burnt valves, too loose causes noise and power loss. The 4M41 uses a rocker-arm design with an adjusting screw and lock nut, making adjustment straightforward. Allow at least 2 hours for the engine to cool before starting.

## Symptoms

- Ticking or clattering noise from the top of the engine, especially when cold
- Noise that disappears or changes as the engine warms up
- Rough idle or slight power loss (if clearance is significantly out of spec)
- Valve train noise at idle that increases with engine speed

## Tools needed

- Feeler gauge set — must include 0.10 mm and 0.15 mm blades
- Open-end spanner 10 mm (lock nut)
- Flat-blade screwdriver (adjusting screw)
- Ratchet and socket set for rocker cover bolts
- Crankshaft turning tool or large socket to rotate engine by hand
- Torque wrench (lock nut tightening)

## Tips

- The engine MUST be cold for accurate measurement. Heat expands metal and will give false readings.
- When in doubt about which direction the camshaft projection is facing, rotate slowly and watch the rockers move — the cylinder that has just completed its intake stroke is on compression TDC.
- If the feeler gauge drops in freely with no resistance, the gap is too wide and needs adjusting.
- After tightening the lock nut, always recheck the clearance — the screw sometimes moves slightly when locked.
- Replace the rocker cover gasket if it shows any hardening, cracking, or signs of leaking.
- The 4M41 in Gen 3 (non-common rail) and Gen 4 (common rail) share the same valve clearance specification.

Replace if motor runs but shift does not complete, or motor does not run at all. $150–350.
Replace if selector turns but sends no signal. $80–180.

The Pajero IV uses a Super Select 4WD system (SS4-II) with four modes: 2H (rear-wheel drive), 4H (part-time 4WD high), 4HLc (4WD high with centre diff locked), and 4LLc (4WD low with centre diff locked). Engagement problems are usually electrical (4WD selector switch, transfer case motor, or wiring) rather than mechanical. The 4WD indicator light on the dashboard confirms the system status — a flashing light indicates an incomplete shift.

## Symptoms

- 4WD indicator light flashing or not illuminating after selector switch change
- Transfer case makes grinding or clicking during mode change
- Vehicle not responding as expected in 4WD (understeer suggests 2H only)
- 4WD engages but low range won't engage (or vice versa)
- Selector dial turns but no mode change occurs
- 4WD light on permanently — system stuck in one mode

## Tips

- For 2H→4H shifts: vehicle must be below 100 km/h. The shift occurs within 1–2 seconds with a light click. If the indicator flashes, the shift is incomplete — stop, select 2H, and try again.
- For 4H→4LLc: stop the vehicle, select Neutral, then move to 4LLc. Never shift to low range while moving.
- A flashing 4WD indicator means the transfer case actuator motor is running but has not completed the shift. Most common causes: speed too high, tyres spinning, or actuator motor fault.
- Front differential engagement on models with locking front diff: the front diff lock indicator should illuminate within 3–5 seconds. Delay usually means the actuator motor is slow or its connector is corroded.
- If 4WD was never used for a long time, the transfer case may be stiff. Engage and disengage several times while driving slowly — this frees up the actuator.
- Check the transfer case fluid — low or degraded fluid causes actuator motor strain and slow shifts.
- The 4WD system uses the ABS wheel speed sensors to detect speed conditions for engagement. ABS faults can prevent 4WD engagement.

Air Conditioning Not Cooling — Diagnosis Guide

The Pajero IV uses a dual-zone automatic climate control system with front and rear outlets. When the AC stops cooling, the cause is usually low refrigerant (slow leak), a failed compressor clutch, a blocked condenser, or a faulty pressure sensor. This guide walks through diagnosing the most common causes before committing to a regas or compressor replacement.

## Symptoms

- Air from vents is warm or only slightly cool
- Cooling works only when driving at speed (condenser airflow-dependent fault)
- AC compressor not cycling on (clutch not engaging)
- AC light illuminated but no cold air
- System blows cold briefly then reverts to warm
- Rear AC not working but front is fine (separate blower motor fault)

## Tips

- Check the cabin air filter first — a blocked filter severely restricts airflow and makes the AC appear to not cool even when the refrigerant charge is correct.
- With engine running and AC on, listen for the compressor clutch engaging — a click from the front of the engine bay followed by a slight RPM drop confirms the compressor is running.
- No click = compressor not engaging. Could be low refrigerant (safety cutout), failed clutch, or blown AC fuse.
- Check the AC fuse in the engine bay fuse box before any other diagnosis — a blown fuse is a 2-minute fix.
- Cooling that works at highway speed but fails in traffic usually means the condenser fan is not running — check the condenser fan fuse and relay.
- If the system cools briefly then stops, the high-pressure cutout is tripping — suspect overcharged refrigerant or a blocked condenser.
- Refrigerant leaks are the most common cause of gradual AC failure on a 10+ year-old vehicle. A UV dye test or electronic sniffer confirms the leak location.
- AC recharges (regassing) are a workshop job requiring certified refrigerant handling equipment — do not attempt to top up with DIY cans on a system that hasn't been leak-tested.
R134a, system capacity 650–750g (verify on underhood sticker)
Exact charge weight is on a sticker near the AC compressor. Do not estimate — overcharge damages the compressor. Workshop only.
Add if compressor or drier replaced
Only required if a component was replaced. Normal regas does not require oil top-up.
Replace any time the AC system is opened to atmosphere. The desiccant saturates with moisture quickly. $40–80.
Replace if compressor is seized or not building pressure. $300–600 remanufactured.

The Pajero IV AC system uses R134a refrigerant. A recharge (regas) restores cooling performance when refrigerant has leaked out over time. This is a workshop job — the system must be evacuated, leak-tested, and charged to the exact specification weight. Overcharging is as damaging as undercharging. Do not use DIY top-up cans without confirming there is no active leak.

## Symptoms

- AC gradually losing cooling performance over months or years
- Compressor cycling rapidly (low-pressure cutout activating)
- AC vents blowing slightly cool but not cold
- Previously recharged system that has lost performance again

## Tips

- Always fix the leak before recharging. A regas on a leaking system is money wasted — the gas will be gone again within weeks or months.
- The refrigerant charge weight label is on a sticker in the engine bay near the AC components. Australian/Middle East variants may differ from European spec.
- If the system has been empty for more than a few months, replace the receiver-drier at the same time — moisture contamination damages the compressor.
- A workshop regas with a proper machine costs $80–150 and includes vacuum test, leak check, and accurate charge. This is the only correct way to recharge AC.
- Clicking or rattling from the AC compressor under load indicates bearing wear — stop using the AC and have the compressor inspected before it seizes and destroys the system.
- Dual-zone systems (front + rear) share the same refrigerant loop but have independent blower motors. If only one zone fails, check that zone's blower before assuming a refrigerant fault.

One of the quickest maintenance tasks on the Pajero IV. A clogged air filter restricts airflow, reduces power, and increases fuel consumption — especially on the 4M41 diesel where the turbo amplifies the effect of restriction. Mitsubishi recommends inspection every 15,000 km and replacement every 30,000 km, or more frequently in dusty conditions (off-road, dirt roads).

## Symptoms

- Routine maintenance interval reached (30,000 km)
- Noticeable drop in performance or throttle response
- Increased fuel consumption
- Black smoke on acceleration (diesel)
- Filter visibly grey/brown with debris when inspected

## Tools needed

- Flat-head screwdriver (airbox clips)
- Soft brush or compressed air (to clean airbox housing)

## Tips

- Inspect the old filter by holding it up to light — if you can't see light through it, replace it.
- Take the opportunity to wipe out the inside of the airbox before fitting the new filter.
- Off-road drivers should inspect and replace more frequently — dusty conditions load filters quickly.
- Performance aftermarket filters (K&N, BMC) are available as a reusable alternative.

Air Flow Sensor — MAF Diagnosis & Cleaning
Hot-wire type MAF sensor, intake manifold mounted
Replace if cleaning does not resolve the fault. $120–250.
Rubber intake hose from air filter to intake manifold
A cracked hose allows unmetered air past the MAF, causing lean code and rough running. $20–40.

The air flow (mass air flow or MAF) sensor measures incoming air volume and density, allowing the ECU to calculate fuel injection duration. A contaminated or failed MAF sensor causes rich running (too much fuel), increased fuel consumption, rough idle, and hesitation on acceleration. Unlike a clogged air filter (obvious restriction), a dirty MAF sends incorrect data to the ECU, so the engine "thinks" more air is entering than actually is. The sensor element is extremely delicate — it must never be touched or sprayed with compressed air, only carefully cleaned with specific solvent.

## Symptoms

- Check Engine light with code P0100 (Air Flow Measurement System Malfunction)
- Check Engine light with code P0101 (Air Flow Measurement System Out of Range)
- Rough, hesitant idle that improves when the engine warms up
- Hesitation on hard acceleration from a stop
- Fuel consumption 15–25% higher than normal
- Engine runs better with the MAF unplugged (limp-home mode using a default air estimate)
- Black soot or carbon buildup visible from the exhaust

## Tools needed

- Socket set (intake manifold bolts)
- MAF sensor cleaning solvent (isopropyl alcohol 90%+ purity ONLY — never use compressed air or water)
- Soft-bristled brush or lint-free wipe (for gentle cleaning)
- OBD-II scanner (to read freeze frame data and confirm MAF readings)

## Tips

- The MAF sensor element is a tiny heated wire that measures air passing through it. If you touch the element with your finger, or spray it with compressed air, you will destroy it instantly — the wire is fragile and permanently damaged by contamination.
- Cleaning procedure: remove the sensor from the intake manifold (2–3 bolts), carefully soak the sensor element in isopropyl alcohol for 5 minutes, then gently shake out excess liquid and allow it to air-dry completely (15+ minutes) before reinstalling.
- Never use water, brake cleaner, or compressed air on the MAF sensor. Isopropyl alcohol is the only safe solvent.
- A MAF sensor that reads out of range (code P0101) may be dirty, but could also indicate an air leak after the MAF (cracked hose, loose clamp). Check the intake hose and clamps before replacing the sensor.
- If the vehicle runs better with the MAF unplugged, the sensor is almost certainly the problem. However, with the MAF disconnected, the engine runs in limp-home mode (fixed air estimate) — this works for a short distance but is inefficient and should be treated as a temporary fix only.
- After cleaning or replacing the MAF, clear the fault codes, then drive a complete cycle (cold start, warm-up, highway, idle, acceleration) for the ECU to relearn air flow patterns. A single test drive may not be enough.
- Intake air temperature sensor (IAT) is often mounted near the MAF. If it becomes contaminated or fails (code P0110), similar symptoms appear. Clean or replace together if both are suspect.

The alternator charges the battery while the engine runs. A failing alternator causes the battery to drain, leading to a no-start condition that is often misdiagnosed as a battery fault. Test the alternator before replacing the battery. Output should be 13.2–14.4V at the battery terminals with the engine running. Replacement requires removing the serpentine belt and is a 1–2 hour job.

## Symptoms

- Battery warning light on dashboard (red battery icon)
- Battery repeatedly going flat despite being new
- Dimming headlights at idle that brighten when engine is revved
- Whining noise from engine bay that changes with RPM
- Electrical accessories (windows, lights) behaving erratically
- Voltmeter reading below 12.5V with engine running

## Tools needed

- Multimeter (DC voltage range)
- Belt tensioner wrench
- 13mm, 14mm sockets
- Torque wrench

## Tips

- Test before replacing: with engine running at idle, battery voltage should be 13.2–14.4V. Rev to 2000 RPM — should climb to 14.0–14.8V.
- If voltage is 12.5–13.0V at idle, the alternator may just have worn brushes — a $20 fix vs $250 for a full unit.
- A whining noise that changes with engine RPM is typically a failing alternator bearing, not brush wear.
- Disconnect the negative battery terminal before removing the alternator — the B+ terminal is live at all times.
- Remanufactured alternators carry a 1–2 year warranty and are 40–60% cheaper than new OEM. Perfectly acceptable for a non-original repair.

The 12V automotive battery powers the starter, alternator, and all electrical systems. Most batteries last 4–6 years before losing capacity, slower cold-start performance, and eventual complete failure. Replacement is one of the simplest maintenance tasks, requiring only basic hand tools and 15 minutes. Modern batteries use lead-acid chemistry and should be recycled — many retailers offer core return credits.

## Symptoms

- Engine cranks slowly (sluggish, weak starter turn)
- Difficulty starting in cold weather (below 5°C)
- Dimming headlights during engine cranking or when running electrical accessories
- Click-click-click when turning the key (starter solenoid cannot pull in due to low voltage)
- Dashboard warning light for battery/alternator (yellow battery icon)
- Battery case is visibly swollen or cracked (internal short or overcharge condition)

## Tools needed

- Open-end or adjustable wrench (10mm or 13mm, depending on terminal bolt size)
- Screwdrivers (flathead and Phillips, for battery hold-down and corrosion removal)
- Wire brush or steel wool (to clean corroded battery terminals)
- Battery terminal puller (optional, if terminals are stuck fast)
- Baking soda and water (to neutralize spilled battery acid)
- Gloves and eye protection (lead-acid batteries contain hazardous material)

## Tips

- Turn off all electrical accessories and the ignition before starting. This reduces the risk of short-circuiting the alternator
- The negative (black) battery terminal should be disconnected FIRST, then the positive (red) terminal. This sequence prevents accidental shorts
- If the battery terminals are heavily corroded (white or blue-green crusty buildup), disconnect and clean them with a wire brush and baking soda slurry before installation
- When installing the new battery, connect the POSITIVE (red) terminal first, then the NEGATIVE (black). This is the reverse of disconnection and minimizes spark risk
- Ensure the battery is sitting flat on the hold-down tray and the bracket is tightened. A loose battery can tip and damage the case or cause intermittent electrical issues
- After installation, start the engine and verify the alternator is charging (voltmeter should read 13.5–14.5V at idle, 14.0–14.8V at 2000 RPM)
- If the engine does not start after battery replacement, or if the new battery discharges overnight, the alternator may be faulty. Test the alternator output before replacing the battery again
- Recycle your old battery at an auto parts store or recycling center. Most retailers offer a $5–$10 core credit, offsetting part of the cost
- Battery terminals can be protected from future corrosion with felt washers or dielectric grease applied to the terminal posts

A strong battery is essential for starting your Pajero IV and powering ancillary systems. Battery capacity (Ah) and cold cranking amperage (CCA) must match your engine's demands. Oversized batteries can overload the alternator; undersized batteries cannot start the engine in cold weather. This guide covers battery selection by engine type.

## Tips

- Always disconnect the negative terminal first before working on electrical systems — this prevents accidental short circuits.
- Invest in a quality battery from a reputable brand (Exide, Lucas, Bosch, Yuasa). Cheap batteries fail prematurely.
- Have your alternator tested if the battery frequently dies or requires charging. A failing alternator cannot keep the battery charged.
- Clean battery terminals every 6 months, especially in humid or coastal climates where corrosion is rapid.
- In very cold climates, use a battery tender (float charger) during winter storage to keep the battery fully charged.
- Never add distilled water to modern sealed batteries — they are maintenance-free.
- If the battery is more than 3 years old and won't hold a charge, replace it rather than trying to revive it.

## Factory procedure reference

- **Factory procedure — Engine Mechanical (Section 11)** (M1, section 11)

The Gen 4 Pajero 3.2 Di-D (4M41 common rail) turbocharger operates with variable-geometry (VGT) boost control. Correct boost pressure is essential for performance, fuel economy, and preventing over-fuelling or under-fuelling. Low boost causes power loss and black smoke; over-boost triggers the wastegate fault code P0049 (Code 49 in blink-code diagnostics) and can indicate a stuck VGT. This page covers the factory boost pressure specifications and how to diagnose a boost problem.

## Symptoms

- Significant power loss under load, especially uphill or towing
- Black smoke from exhaust during acceleration
- Check Engine Light with code P0049 (over-boost) or boost sensor codes
- Turbo spools up but no increase in power after a certain point
- Turbo whistle that is unusually loud or high-pitched

## Tips

- A split intercooler hose is the most common cause of a sudden power loss on the 4M41. Pull over and check all hoses before assuming a turbo failure.
- Oil in the intercooler (visible black oily residue inside the hoses or intercooler tank) is normal in small quantities — it comes from crankcase blow-by. Large quantities indicate a failing turbo oil seal.
- VGT actuators on older Gen 4 trucks can stick from carbon and rust. Some owners clean and lubricate the actuator mechanism to restore free movement.
- After any intercooler hose repair, the vehicle needs to relearn boost control — a short drive with several full-throttle accelerations will help the ECU adapt.
Mitsubishi OEM brake fluid or Castrol, Mobil, Shell equivalents
Capacity: Approximately 2 liters for complete system flush. Buy premium DOT 4 for longer intervals

MD619269 or similar, depending on model year
Rare, but sensor can fail and trigger warning light despite adequate fluid level

Snug but not forceful. Bleeder valves are delicate and strip easily. Back out only 1/2 turn to 3/4 turn to avoid draining threads

Brake fluid is hygroscopic — it absorbs water from the air. Over time, dissolved water lowers the boiling point of the fluid, reducing brake performance, especially under heavy use (towing, mountain passes). Factory service intervals recommend a complete brake fluid flush every 2 years or 40,000 km. This procedure involves bleeding each wheel cylinder or caliper to remove old fluid and replace it with fresh DOT 3 or DOT 4 fluid. Proper brake bleeding order is critical: RR, LR, RF, LF (rear-right, rear-left, front-right, front-left) to avoid air pockets.

## Symptoms

- Scheduled maintenance interval (every 2 years or 40,000 km)
- Brake pedal feels spongy or soft (indicates air in lines or water-soaked fluid)
- Brake fluid appears dark brown or has a burnt smell (oxidation and water contamination)
- Brakes fade during towing or mountain descents (fluid boils, reducing pressure)
- Brake warning light on dashboard (low fluid level or pressure imbalance detected)
- Visible brake fluid leakage from wheel cylinders or brake lines

## Tools needed

- Brake bleeder wrench or box wrench (usually 10mm, fits bleeder valve on caliper/cylinder)
- Bleeder hose and catch bottle to collect old fluid
- Manual pressure bleeder or vacuum pump (optional, but highly recommended for speed)
- Brake fluid catch pan to prevent spills on paint
- Rags and shop towels (brake fluid eats paint and plastic, clean spills immediately)
- Flashlight and a helper (two people recommended, though solo is possible with care)

## Tips

- Brake fluid eats paint and plastic — wipe spills immediately with a damp cloth. Do NOT allow brake fluid to sit on the vehicle's finish
- If you do not have a helper, use a one-way brake bleeder valve (bought separately, fits on the bleeder screw) to prevent backflow. Or use a manual pressure bleeder (bottle with hose that applies steady pressure while you open bleeder valves)
- Always open the bleeder valve BEFORE pressing the pedal, and close it BEFORE releasing the pedal. If you release the pedal with the valve open, air will be drawn back into the system
- Some people use a turkey baster or syringe to suck old fluid from the master cylinder reservoir before bleeding. This speeds up the process
- Check the fluid level in the master cylinder often. If it runs dry, air enters the system and you must start over. Refill as needed
- The last wheel to bleed (LF, front-left) often takes longest because it is farthest from the master cylinder and air moves slowly through the long line
- After bleeding all wheels, have a helper press the brake pedal hard while you check for leaks at all four calipers/cylinders and at the master cylinder. Tighten any wet bleeder valves
- Dispose of old brake fluid as hazardous waste — many auto shops accept it for free, or take it to a recycling center
- Modern ABS-equipped vehicles may require a special bleeding procedure to activate the ABS module. Consult the factory manual for your specific year/model

The Pajero IV has four flexible rubber brake hoses — one per corner — connecting the rigid brake lines to the brake callipers and rear axle. Rubber hoses degrade internally over time, bulging or collapsing under pressure. A collapsed hose can cause brake drag on one wheel (vehicle pulls to one side) or slow brake release. Inspect all four hoses at major services. Replace in axle pairs when any sign of deterioration is found.

## Symptoms

- Vehicle pulls to one side during braking (restricted hose on that side)
- Brake drag on one wheel — wheel hot after driving, brakes not fully releasing
- Visible cracking, bubbling, or swelling of the rubber hose body
- Soft or spongy brake pedal (internal hose collapse restricting fluid return)
- Brake fluid leak at hose-to-line or hose-to-calliper connection

## Tools needed

- 10mm and 11mm flare-nut spanner (brake line fittings)
- 14mm open spanner (hose-to-calliper banjo bolt)
- Brake hose holding tool or locking pliers
- Brake bleeder kit
- Torque wrench

## Tips

- Always use a flare-nut spanner on brake line fittings — an open-ended spanner rounds the soft brass nut immediately.
- Hold the hose body with a second spanner when tightening the line nut — do not allow the hose to twist.
- Inspect hoses with the suspension at full droop and full compression — a hose that is tight at full droop may be cracking at the bend.
- Replace copper banjo bolt sealing washers on both sides every time the banjo is disturbed — they are one-use crush washers.
- Bleed the affected calliper after any hose replacement. If you replaced a front hose, bleed that corner. Rear hose replacement requires bleeding both rear callipers.
- Never re-use brake fluid drained from the system — brake fluid absorbs moisture and should be discarded.

Brake noise (squealing, grinding, creaking) and poor feel (soft pedal, extended stopping distance) are often signs of worn pads, moisture in fluid, or air in the brake lines. While some noise is cosmetic, grinding and a soft pedal indicate safety issues. Diagnosis is straightforward: inspect pads for wear, check fluid condition, and bleed air if the pedal is soft.

## Symptoms

- High-pitched squealing when braking (usually front pads, often ceramic pads are louder)
- Grinding sound and metal-on-metal feel (brake pads worn to backing plate)
- Soft or spongy brake pedal (air in lines or moisture in fluid)
- Extended stopping distance (degraded pads, moisture in fluid, or air in system)
- Brake pedal fades during repeated hard braking (fluid boiling due to high temperature)
- Uneven braking (one wheel locks up before others, or vehicle pulls to one side)
- Creaking noise when braking (suspension or brake caliper movement, not pad wear)

## Tools needed

- Jack and jack stands (to safely lift vehicle)
- Wheel chocks (to prevent rolling)
- Socket set for caliper bolts
- Brake fluid (DOT 3 or DOT 4)
- Bleed kit or manual pump (if bleeding brakes)

## Tips

- Brake noise is usually not a safety issue unless it's grinding (metal-on-metal). Squealing is annoying but your brakes still work
- Brake pads are a wear item; expect to replace them every 40,000-80,000 km depending on driving style (frequent hard braking wears pads faster)
- Brake rotors often last 100,000+ km if pads are changed before metal touches rotor. Let pads wear too long and rotors will need replacement too
- Moisture in brake fluid lowers boiling point from 205°C (dry) to 140°C (saturated). This is why fluid flush is recommended every 2 years
- If your brakes feel different after a repair, it may just be new pads bedding in (first 100 km). Pedal feel will normalize

Front brake pads on the Pajero IV typically last 40,000–60,000 km depending on driving style. They must always be replaced as a complete axle set (both sides). The process requires winding back the front caliper pistons — a simple piston push-in type, not screw-in.

## Symptoms

- Squealing or squeaking noise under braking
- Grinding metal-on-metal sound when braking
- Longer stopping distances than usual
- Brake warning light illuminated
- Pad friction material worn to less than 2mm
- Brake dust visible inside wheel dramatically reduced (worn pads produce less dust)

## Tools needed

- 17mm socket (wheel bolts)
- 12mm + 14mm socket (caliper bolts)
- Caliper piston compression tool (C-clamp or dedicated tool)
- Wire brush (for caliper slides)
- Brake cleaner spray
- Torque wrench

## Tips

- Always replace pads as an axle set (both sides). Replacing one side creates uneven braking.
- Remove about 20–30ml of fluid from the reservoir before compressing pistons to prevent overflow.
- Clean the caliper slide pins and regrease them — seized pins are a common cause of uneven wear and poor fuel economy.
- Bed in new pads with 8–10 moderate stops from 60 km/h before any hard braking.
- Do not apply grease to the friction material — only to backing plate edges and slide pin contact points.

The Pajero IV uses disc brakes on all four wheels. Rear pads typically last 60,000–80,000 km depending on driving style. Rear caliper pistons rotate as they compress (unlike front pistons which press straight in), so you need a brake piston wind-back tool — a screwdriver will not work and will damage the caliper. Always replace rear pads as a complete axle set (both sides).

## Symptoms

- Squealing or grinding noise from rear brakes
- Brake pad warning light illuminated (if rear sensors fitted)
- Rear pad thickness below 3mm when inspected
- Vehicle pulling to one side under braking
- Pulsing or vibration through brake pedal

## Tools needed

- 17mm socket (wheel bolts)
- 12mm + 14mm socket (caliper bolts)
- Brake piston wind-back tool (cube/socket type for rotating pistons — essential)
- Torque wrench
- Wire brush (caliper slides)

## Tips

- The rear caliper pistons ROTATE as they compress — you cannot push them straight in with a clamp or screwdriver. A wind-back tool is mandatory.
- Wind-back direction: clockwise when viewed from behind the piston (check your specific caliper — direction markings are usually moulded in).
- Clean the caliper slide pins with a wire brush and apply fresh brake lubricant. Seized slides are the most common cause of uneven rear pad wear.
- Before compressing pistons, remove about 50ml of fluid from the reservoir — the pistons displacing back will overflow the reservoir if it's full.
- Bed in new pads with 10–15 moderate stops from 60 km/h before any hard braking. This seats the pad compound to the disc surface.

First thing to try. $2–5 at any supermarket or pharmacy. Replace every 2–3 years.

Central locking and remote key faults on the Pajero IV fall into two categories: remote key (keyfob/transponder) issues and the physical locking actuator system. A dead key battery is the most common cause of remote failure. Actuator faults affect individual doors. The ETACS module (Electronic Time and Alarm Control System, Section 52 of the service manual) governs the entire body electronics including locking, alarm, and immobiliser.

## Symptoms

- Remote key not working (no lock/unlock response)
- One door not locking or unlocking with central locking
- Alarm triggering unexpectedly or not arming
- Immobiliser light flashing — engine won't start
- Central locking works from inside switch but not from remote
- Doors locking but not unlocking (or vice versa)

## Tips

- Replace the remote key battery first (CR2032 coin cell). This fixes 60% of remote key complaints. Key should respond within 1 metre of the vehicle after battery change.
- After replacing the key battery, you may need to re-sync the remote: sit in the vehicle, insert key in ignition, press lock button 5 times within 10 seconds, then press unlock once. Confirm with a lock/unlock cycle.
- If only one door fails, the fault is the door actuator motor for that door — not the ETACS module or remote.
- The Pajero IV immobiliser uses a transponder chip in the key. A worn or damaged key may start the engine intermittently. Have the key copied before it fails completely.
- Check fuse 12 (20A) in the engine bay fuse box for the ETACS/body control system if all locking functions fail simultaneously.
- Water ingress in door actuators is common on older vehicles. A door actuator that works intermittently in dry weather but fails after rain usually has a corroded motor.
- Alarm false triggers are often caused by a faulty bonnet/boot switch — the alarm thinks the bonnet is being opened. Spray the switch contacts with contact cleaner.

The Check Engine Light (CEL), also called the Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL), illuminates whenever the engine ECU detects an emission or engine performance fault. The light alone does not tell you what the problem is — you need a diagnostic scanner to read the fault code. Some faults are minor (faulty oxygen sensor) and car remains drivable; others are serious (timing retarded, misfire) and should be addressed immediately. This guide explains the different types of CEL conditions and how to diagnose them.

## Symptoms

- Check Engine Light illuminated on the instrument cluster (either steady or blinking)
- Blinking/flashing light = active misfire (severe condition, stop driving immediately)
- Steady light = fault logged, vehicle is drivable (inspect for secondary symptoms)
- Reduced engine power or limp-mode (ECU cuts cylinders or limits boost to protect engine)
- Rough idle, hesitation, or poor acceleration
- Increased fuel consumption or black smoke
- No apparent symptoms (many electrical faults trigger CEL without noticeable change)

## Tools needed

- OBD2 diagnostic scanner (loaned free at auto parts stores or purchased for 30-60 dollars)
- Flashlight and screwdrivers to inspect engine for loose wires and corrosion

## Tips

- Do NOT clear the Check Engine Light without understanding the fault. Many shops clear codes and take your money without fixing anything. The light comes back shortly after
- Some codes are intermittent. You may see a code like P0171 (lean) logged, but the condition clears itself if you drive on the highway. This is often a loose connector or a sensor entering/exiting a failure window
- [object Object]
- Avoid disconnecting the battery to clear the CEL. This erases all diagnostic history and makes troubleshooting harder. Use a scanner to clear codes after fixing the fault
- [object Object]
- Professional diagnosis costs $100–$200 at an independent shop but saves money vs. guessing and replacing parts randomly

Replace if fluid leaks at the gearbox end. Requires gearbox removal on some configurations. $40–100.

Manual transmission Pajero IV models use a hydraulic clutch with a master cylinder at the pedal and a slave cylinder at the gearbox. Air in the hydraulic circuit causes a spongy pedal and incomplete clutch release — symptoms identical to a worn clutch plate. Bleeding removes air from the circuit. Pedal height and freeplay also have factory specifications and affect clutch feel and release point. These checks should be part of any clutch complaint diagnosis before assuming mechanical wear.

## Symptoms

- Spongy or soft clutch pedal (air in hydraulic circuit)
- Clutch not fully releasing — hard to select gears from rest (air or low fluid)
- Clutch pedal height changed or feels different to normal
- Clutch fluid level dropping in reservoir (leak in master or slave cylinder)
- Clutch drag after fluid change or any hydraulic work

## Tools needed

- Clutch bleeding kit or vacuum bleeder
- 8mm spanner (bleed nipple on slave cylinder)
- Clear plastic tube and catch bottle
- Torque wrench

## Tips

- Check the clutch fluid reservoir before bleeding — it is usually the same reservoir as the brake master cylinder, or a separate small reservoir on the firewall near the clutch pedal. Level should be between MIN and MAX.
- Bleeding procedure: attach a clear tube to the slave cylinder bleed nipple, open the nipple 3/4 turn, then pump the clutch pedal slowly to the floor and hold it. Close the nipple before releasing the pedal. Repeat until no bubbles appear in the fluid stream.
- Never let the reservoir run dry while bleeding — air drawn in at the master cylinder will require you to start over.
- Clutch pedal height (from floor to pedal pad): factory specification is approximately 170–180 mm. Adjust via the pedal stopper bolt behind the pedal arm.
- Clutch pedal freeplay (slack before resistance is felt): approximately 1–3 mm. Adjust via the pushrod at the master cylinder.
- If the clutch still drags after a thorough bleed with no air in the system, the fault is mechanical — worn pressure plate, bent clutch plate, or worn release bearing.
- DOT 3/4 brake fluid absorbs moisture over time. If the fluid is more than 2 years old, replace it completely during the bleed rather than just topping up.

OEM coil for 6G75. $60–120 each. Replace the faulty one, or all 6 for peace of mind at 100,000+ km.

The 6G75 petrol engine uses six individual coil-on-plug ignition coils, one per cylinder. Each coil sits directly on top of its spark plug. A failing coil causes a single-cylinder misfire (P0301–P0306) and rough running. Unlike older distributor systems, you can swap coils between cylinders to confirm a fault — if the misfire code follows the coil, the coil is bad. This is a petrol-only job; the 4M41 diesel has no spark ignition.

## Symptoms

- P0301–P0306 misfire code (specific cylinder)
- Rough idle or engine shaking
- Hesitation or stumble at low RPM
- Check Engine light illuminated
- Symptoms worse when cold or damp

## Tools needed

- 10mm socket (coil retaining bolt)
- Electrical connector removal tool (or small flat-head)
- OBD-II scanner (to read and clear misfire codes)

## Tips

- To confirm a bad coil without buying parts: swap the suspect coil with an adjacent one and rescan. If P0302 becomes P0303, the coil is confirmed faulty.
- Inspect the coil boot (rubber tip) for cracks or carbon tracking — a cracked boot can cause a misfire without the coil itself failing.
- Coils are prone to failure after water ingress (engine washing, heavy rain). Misfire codes appearing after wet weather often mean a failed coil.
- At 100,000+ km, consider replacing all 6 coils as a set. The labour to reach them is the same whether you replace one or six.
- Always replace spark plugs at the same time as coils — worn plugs stress coils and cause premature failure.

Engine coolant degrades over time as additives break down and corrosion products accumulate. Factory intervals call for a complete coolant flush every 40,000 km or every 2 years, whichever comes first. Neglecting this allows scale buildup in the radiator, water jacket, and heater core, reducing cooling efficiency and promoting rust inside the engine block. Both 4M41 diesel and 6G75 petrol use the same coolant specification.

## Symptoms

- Scheduled maintenance interval (40,000 km or 2 years)
- [object Object]
- Overheating or slow warm-up during winter
- Sweet smell from the exhaust (coolant leak into combustion chamber) or from engine bay
- Visible corrosion or white crusty deposits on radiator fins or hoses
- Heater not blowing hot air despite engine temperature being normal

## Tools needed

- Pan or drain container (minimum 10 liters capacity)
- Screwdriver or wrench for radiator drain plug (usually Phillips head or 10mm hex)
- Large funnel with a filter or cloth strainer
- Soft hose or siphon (to drain hard-to-reach areas if needed)
- Coolant mixer or distilled water (to dilute concentrate if using concentrate)
- Thermometer (optional, to verify final coolant temperature)

## Tips

- Park the vehicle on level ground and allow the engine to cool completely (at least 1 hour) before starting work. Hot coolant can scald.
- The radiator drain plug is on the bottom driver's side of the radiator. Locate it before draining; it may be hidden behind a splash guard.
- Drain the radiator first (10 minutes), then drain the coolant reservoir if accessible (held by a clip, pops off easily).
- After draining, you may need to remove the lower radiator hose and squeeze its contents into the drain pan.
- [object Object]
- Refill with 50/50 premixed coolant (or concentrate + distilled water mixed in equal parts). Do not use tap water, which contains minerals that cause scale.
- Run the engine with the radiator cap off for 5–10 minutes to purge air bubbles from the system. You will see bubbles rising. Top up as needed.
- Reinstall the radiator cap only after the engine has cooled completely.
- Check the coolant level again after 100 km of driving. Thermal expansion will draw in more coolant if needed.
- Some vehicles have a bleed screw on the thermostat housing (top of engine). If the system is hard to fill (lots of air), open this screw briefly while filling to release trapped air.

Coolant prevents your engine from overheating and protects against corrosion and freezing. The Pajero IV cooling system holds approximately 9.5 liters and requires a specific type of coolant compatible with Mitsubishi's aluminum engine blocks and radiators. Using the wrong coolant can cause corrosion, leaks, and engine damage. This guide covers coolant selection, capacity, and flush intervals for both the 4M41 diesel and 6G75 petrol engines.

## Tips

- Always let the engine cool before opening the radiator cap — pressurized coolant can cause severe burns.
- Check coolant level in the expansion tank (not the radiator directly). Top up with the same type if low.
- Never use water alone as coolant — it freezes in winter and lacks corrosion inhibitors.
- A 50/50 mix of coolant and distilled water is standard for most climates. In very cold regions (-30°C or below), use 60% coolant / 40% water.
- If your coolant level drops frequently, you have a leak. Do not ignore this — a running engine without coolant overheats in seconds.
- White crusty deposits on the engine or radiator indicate coolant leaks or a failed gasket. Have the system inspected.
- When flushing, use distilled water to rinse the system, not tap water. Tap water contains minerals that corrode.

Inductive proximity sensor, cylinder block mounted
Replace if multimeter shows no AC voltage output with engine cranking. $50–120.
Inductive sensor, same function as 4M41 but different connector
Replace if corroded or pins are bent. $10–20.

The crank angle sensor (CAS) reads the crankshaft position and speed, providing the ECU with timing information for fuel injection and ignition. A failed CAS causes immediate no-start or stalling, as the ECU cannot determine when to fire injectors or ignition. Unlike oxygen sensors that degrade gradually, the CAS either works or doesn't — there is no "weak" state. Symptoms are sudden, not progressive. The sensor is located on the engine block near the crankshaft pulley and can be tested with a multimeter and an oscilloscope (workshop tools).

## Symptoms

- Engine will not start (cranks but no ignition or fuel)
- Engine dies immediately after starting (cannot maintain sync)
- Stalling at random speeds or idle (intermittent sensor signal loss)
- Check Engine light with code P0335 (Crankshaft Position Sensor Circuit)
- Check Engine light with code P0336 (Crankshaft Position Sensor Signal Out of Range)
- Rough idle or hesitation on acceleration (weak or unstable signal)

## Tools needed

- Multimeter (to test sensor output voltage)
- Oscilloscope (for detailed waveform analysis — workshop tool)
- 10mm or 12mm socket (sensor mounting bolt)
- OBD-II scanner (to read fault codes and sensor data)

## Tips

- The CAS is tested by cranking the engine and measuring AC voltage at the sensor connector (usually 0.3–2V AC, depending on engine speed). If cranking produces zero voltage, the sensor is dead.
- Do not test with the key ON (engine off) — the sensor produces no signal without crankshaft rotation. Always crank the engine to see a signal.
- If the multimeter shows AC voltage but the engine still won't start, the problem is not the CAS — the issue is in the wiring to the ECU or the ECU itself.
- A corroded sensor connector is a common cause of intermittent no-start. Check the connector at the sensor and at the ECU. Clean terminals with electrical contact cleaner and apply dielectric grease.
- The sensor gap (distance from the sensor tip to the crankshaft timing ring) is critical: typically 0.3–0.5 mm. If the sensor was removed during another repair and reinstalled too far away, it will not read the crankshaft signal. Check gap before condemning a new sensor.
- If replacing the CAS, mark the original sensor's angular position (e.g., pointing at the 12 o'clock position on the block) before removal. Reinstall the new sensor in the same position to avoid further timing issues.
- After replacing the CAS, clear the Check Engine codes and drive a full cycle (cold start, warm-up, highway speeds) to allow the ECU to relearn. The fault may re-appear if timing is not correct.

The Pajero IV has front CV (constant velocity) joints and rear propshaft universal joints. The front CV joints allow the wheels to steer while transmitting drive. They are packed with grease inside rubber boots — when a boot tears, grease escapes, grit enters, and the joint destroys itself within weeks. Inspect boots at every 20,000 km service. Clicking or clunking when turning sharply at low speed is a classic sign of a worn outer CV joint.

## Symptoms

- Clicking or clunking sound when turning sharply at low speed (outer CV joint)
- Vibration through steering wheel or floor at highway speed (front driveshaft balance)
- Grease flung onto wheel or inner guard (torn CV boot)
- Vibration through floor increasing with speed (rear propshaft U-joint)
- Knocking from underneath during acceleration from rest (worn inner CV or U-joint)
- Torn rubber boot visible on front driveshaft

## Tools needed

- 17mm socket (wheel bolts)
- 30mm socket or equivalent (hub nut — if removing driveshaft)
- CV boot clamp pliers
- CV joint grease (molybdenum disulphide type)
- Torque wrench

## Tips

- Inspect CV boots at every oil change — crouch beside the front wheel and look at the driveshaft for grease spray or visible boot damage.
- A torn boot with no clicking yet means the joint is still good — replace the boot and regrease before the joint wears. This is a $30 fix vs $150+ for a new joint.
- Clicking on full lock in one direction only indicates the outer CV joint on that side. Clicking in both directions usually means an inner joint.
- Do not confuse CV joint clicking with brake pad wear indicator squeal — brake squeal is continuous at low speed, CV click is only when turning.
- Off-road driving over rocks and obstacles tears CV boots rapidly. Check them after every off-road trip.

Head gasket failure is uncommon on the Pajero IV but can occur after overheating events or at very high mileage (200,000+ km). The 4M41 diesel and 6G75 petrol both use multi-layer steel (MLS) head gaskets. Diagnosis is more important than replacement — head gasket work is a major workshop job, and many symptoms attributed to the head gasket have other causes (coolant loss is usually a hose or cap; white smoke is often diesel injection). Confirm thoroughly before condemning the gasket.

## Symptoms

- White smoke from exhaust that persists after engine is warm (coolant burning)
- Milky or mayonnaise-like contamination on the oil filler cap (coolant in oil)
- Coolant level dropping regularly with no external leak found
- Bubbles visible in coolant reservoir when engine is running (combustion gases)
- Engine overheating without obvious coolant loss or hose failure
- Oil contaminated in coolant reservoir (oil floating on top)

## Tips

- Check the oil filler cap first — a small amount of white condensation inside is normal in a cold climate. Thick, creamy, mayonnaise-like substance throughout the cap and filler neck is a genuine concern.
- A chemical block test (combustion gas test kit, available at auto stores) is the most reliable non-invasive test. Add the tester fluid to the coolant reservoir; if it changes from blue to yellow, combustion gases are present.
- White smoke on cold start that clears within 2–3 minutes is condensation — normal. Persistent white smoke after the engine is warm and up to temperature is the diagnostic sign to watch.
- If coolant is disappearing but there is no external leak, no white smoke, no milky oil, and no bubbles in the reservoir — suspect the heater core before the head gasket.
- On the 4M41 diesel, white smoke at idle after warmup is more likely an injector leak-off problem than a head gasket — check injector return lines first.
- Head gasket replacement requires removing the cylinder head, checking the head surface for warping (maximum 0.05 mm flatness deviation), and resurfacing if needed. This is a workshop job.
- If the engine has overheated, do not continue running it — coolant loss from a blown gasket leads to rapid cylinder wall and bearing damage.

Quick reference for dashboard warning lights on the Pajero IV. Red lights require immediate attention — stop safely as soon as possible. Amber lights indicate a fault that needs diagnosis but is not immediately dangerous. Blue and green lights are informational. When in doubt, connect an OBD-II scanner to read the fault code before driving further.

## Symptoms

- Warning light illuminated on dashboard
- Multiple warning lights on after startup (may be a battery or sensor fault)
- Warning light on but vehicle appears to drive normally

## Tips

- RED warning lights: stop the vehicle as soon as it is safe. Oil pressure, engine temperature, and brake warning lights indicate conditions that cause immediate engine or safety damage.
- AMBER warning lights: continue driving cautiously to a workshop. Check Engine, ABS, traction control, and suspension lights should be scanned before next long journey.
- All lights illuminating at startup and then going off is normal — this is a bulb check. Lights staying on after 3 seconds indicate a fault.
- Check Engine light (MIL): connect an OBD-II scanner to read the fault code. The light alone tells you nothing — the code tells you everything.
- ABS light on without Check Engine: usually a wheel speed sensor fault. Safe to drive but ABS is disabled.
- Battery light on while driving: alternator is not charging. Drive directly to a workshop — the battery will go flat within 20–40 minutes.
- Oil pressure light on while driving: stop immediately, do not restart. Check oil level. If oil level is correct, the oil pump or pressure sensor may have failed.
- Glow plug light (diesel) staying on or flashing: indicates a glow plug circuit fault. Cold starting will be affected. See P0380 fault code guide.
- DPF light (diesel): exhaust filter needs regeneration. Take a 30-minute motorway drive immediately. If light remains, workshop regeneration required.
- TPMS light (tyre pressure): check all four tyre pressures including spare. Light resets when pressures are corrected and vehicle is driven above 25 km/h.
Di-D Diesel — Reading Fault Codes Without a Scan Tool (Blink Code Method)

The Mitsubishi Pajero 3.2 Di-D diesel has a built-in self-diagnostic system that can output fault codes through flashing dashboard warning lights — no scan tool, no MUT III, no laptop required. By grounding a single pin in the OBD connector, the ECU enters diagnostic mode and blinks out codes for the engine, automatic gearbox, transfer case, and ABS systems simultaneously through their respective warning lights. This method works for a preliminary diagnosis; a proper scan tool gives live data and more detail, but for a quick code read this procedure costs nothing.

## Symptoms

- Check Engine Light illuminated
- ABS warning light on
- Transfer case / 4WD warning light on
- Automatic gearbox warning light on

## Tools needed

- Short piece of wire or paperclip to bridge OBD terminal No. 1 to ground
- Pen and paper to record flash sequences

## Tips

- Take a video of the flashing lights on your phone — it is much easier to count flashes from a recording than in real time.
- If the Check Engine Light does not flash at all (stays solid or off) after grounding the diagnostic pin, check your ground connection.
- Code 12 appearing alone in the engine channel means no engine faults. Do not confuse it with Code 12 for the boost pressure sensor — they are the same number.
- This procedure gives stored codes only — it does not give live sensor data. For intermittent faults, a proper OBD2 scan tool with Mitsubishi-specific support is needed.
- Standard generic OBD2 readers often cannot read Mitsubishi-specific codes on the gearbox and transfer case — they only read generic SAE engine codes.

The front and rear differentials contain hypoid gear oil that lubricates the ring and pinion gears and the differential carrier. Over time, the oil oxidizes and metallic particles from gear wear accumulate, reducing lubricant viscosity and protective film thickness. Factory intervals call for replacement every 40,000 km under normal driving, or every 20,000 km for towing or off-road use. Neglecting differential oil changes causes accelerated gear wear, whining noises, and eventual catastrophic failure.

## Symptoms

- Scheduled maintenance interval (40,000 km normal, 20,000 km if towing/off-road)
- Whining or howling from the differential during acceleration or coasting
- Metallic grinding noise when turning (suggests gear damage)
- Visible contamination in oil when draining (metal shavings, sludge)
- Oil overfilled or low level (indicates internal leakage or overflow)
- Sudden loss of traction or clunking on hard acceleration

## Tools needed

- Axle/differential drain pan (minimum 3 liters capacity)
- Wrench or socket for drain plugs (usually 17mm or 19mm hex)
- Wrench or socket for filler plugs (usually 24mm)
- New crush washers or gaskets (buy with the oil)
- Hand pump or electric oil pump (to refill the differential — very slow with a funnel)
- Clean rags or shop towels (to wipe plugs and sealing surfaces)
- Jack and jack stands (to safely lift the vehicle off the ground)

## Tips

- The front differential is on the front axle, under the engine bay. The rear differential is visible on the rear axle assembly, mounted to the frame.
- If the vehicle has multiple transfer case settings (2H, 4H, 4L), select 2H (high-range 2WD) before starting work. Do not change gears while the vehicle is elevated.
- Drain the front differential first, then the rear. Let each drain for at least 10 minutes to ensure full evacuation.
- The filler plug is located above the drain plug (on the side of the differential housing). Once drained, remove this plug to check the level after refilling.
- Filling via a regular funnel is tedious; an electric oil pump with a tube makes the job 10x faster. Pour the oil into the pump bottle, position the tube in the filler hole, and cycle the pump.
- The differential should be filled until oil just reaches the bottom of the filler plug hole. Use a straight edge or rod to measure this precisely.
- Reinstall the filler plug before the drain plug is fully tightened — this ensures you can remove it for level verification.
- After refilling, idle the vehicle in neutral for 1–2 minutes, then drive gently for 10 km. Recheck the level; it may need topping up if air pockets were purged.
- Some enthusiasts use a catch-can to capture a small oil sample during draining. Examine it under light for the volume and size of metal particles — this indicates gear wear.
- If the differential oil is severely contaminated (black, chunky sludge) or metallic particles are visible, a deeper inspection or gearbox overhaul may be warranted.

Steel pin with nylon bushing, press fit into hinge

Door sag, wind noise, and poor sealing on the Pajero IV are usually caused by hinge wear or striker misalignment rather than structural damage. The factory manual documents adjustment procedures for door clearance, door height, hood (bonnet) height and striker engagement, and the back door (tailgate). All are adjustable within a range using slotted mounting points — no specialist tools required. Door handle play checks are also part of the Body section.

## Symptoms

- Door drops when opened and does not align with the body when closing
- Door requires lifting to close properly (sagging hinge)
- Wind noise when driving — door not seating fully in the seal
- Door striker clicking or chattering over bumps (striker engagement too loose)
- Tailgate (back door) difficult to close or has excessive play
- Hood (bonnet) sitting higher on one side or not latching flush
- Door handle has excessive free travel before triggering the latch

## Tools needed

- T40 Torx socket (hinge bolts on some models)
- 13mm socket (striker bolts)
- Marker pen (to mark striker contact point)
- Torque wrench

## Tips

- Door sag is caused by worn hinge bushings or pins. Before adjusting the striker, check the hinges: open the door to 45 degrees and lift the door edge — if it moves more than 3 mm vertically, the hinge pins or bushings are worn and should be replaced before adjusting the striker.
- Striker adjustment: the door striker is on a slotted plate and can be moved up/down and in/out. Loosen the striker bolts, close the door slowly and observe where the latch contacts the striker, then shift the striker to centre the contact. Use a marker pen on the striker to see the contact point.
- Hood adjustment: the hood hinge is bolted to the body with slotted holes — loosen both hinge bolts on the affected side and reposition. The hood striker (the pin the latch hooks over) is also adjustable for height — turn it in or out to achieve flush fitment.
- Back door fit: the tailgate hinges, striker, and check strap are all adjustable. If the back door droops when open, the check strap (the device that holds the door at full-open) may be stretched or the hinge bushings worn.
- Door seal compression: a correctly adjusted door compresses the seal evenly around the perimeter. If you can pull a piece of paper out from between the closed door and the seal without resistance, the door is not sealing at that point — adjust the striker inward.
- Door handle play: the factory specification for outside handle play is 0–2 mm of free movement before the latch begins to open. If handle play is greater than this, the handle-to-latch rod may be out of adjustment or the handle mechanism is worn.
- After any door adjustment, always test the door lock and unlock function before reassembling trim — the central locking actuator rod routing can be disturbed if the door card is removed.

Laminated door glass, clip-mount to regulator
Replace only if cracked or chipped. Adjustment resolves most fit issues. $100–250.

Door glass problems on the Pajero IV fall into two categories: mechanical (glass misalignment, sliding mechanism wear, regulator failure) and sealing (water ingress, wind noise, worn door seals). The factory manual covers glass adjustment, sliding mechanism checks, and door fit adjustment. Most door glass issues can be diagnosed and corrected without replacing components — adjustment resolves more problems than replacement.

## Symptoms

- Wind noise from door glass at highway speed (glass not seating fully in the seal)
- Water ingress at the door glass surround or door seal (torn or compressed seal)
- Door window glass not travelling to the full-up or full-down position (regulator or motor)
- Glass moves slowly or jerks during operation (worn regulator mechanism or weak motor)
- Glass sits proud of the door frame at the top when fully closed (misalignment)
- Rattling from the door glass over rough roads (loose regulator or glass clips)

## Tools needed

- Trim removal tool (to remove door card)
- T30 Torx socket (window regulator bolts)
- 10mm socket

## Tips

- Glass alignment adjustment: in most cases, glass misalignment is corrected by loosening the regulator mounting bolts inside the door (requires removing the door card), repositioning the glass, and retightening. No parts needed.
- To remove the door card: prise off the armrest screw covers, remove the screws, then run a trim tool around the perimeter to release the plastic clips. The window switch harness disconnects with a press-tab connector.
- Water ingress test: with the door closed, run a garden hose over the door glass and surrounding seals. Have someone inside observe where water enters. This pinpoints whether the problem is the glass seal, door seal, or drain holes blocked.
- Door drain holes: there are usually 2–3 drain holes in the bottom of the door skin. These can become blocked with road debris. Water in the door cavity is normal — it must drain out. Clear blocked holes with a piece of wire.
- Wind noise at the top corner of the door glass is almost always the outer weatherstrip lifting away from the glass. Press the seal back against the glass and run your hand along it — if it seals the noise momentarily, the seal needs replacing or re-adhering.
- If the regulator motor runs but the glass does not move, the regulator mechanism (the scissor arms or cable system) has broken. The motor and regulator are usually sold as a combined unit.

DPF replacement (if physically blocked or damaged)
Very expensive ($1,500–$2,500 OEM). Avoid replacement unless filter is cracked or totally blocked

The Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF) traps soot from the 4M41 diesel exhaust and periodically regenerates (burns soot at high temperature) to clean itself. If you mostly drive short distances at low speeds, the engine never gets hot enough for regeneration, and soot accumulates. The ECU monitors DPF loading and illuminates a warning light when it exceeds 90%. Many owners can resolve this with a single long highway drive; others may need dealer intervention. Understanding DPF regeneration cycles helps you maintain this system and avoid costly repairs.

## Symptoms

- Orange or amber warning light on dashboard (Emissions, DPF, or Engine light)
- Reduced engine power (ECU enters limp-mode to reduce soot production)
- Black smoke from the exhaust during acceleration (soot being partially burned)
- Loud fan noise from the rear undercarriage (cooling fans for DPF casing)
- Increased fuel consumption or rough idle
- Error codes P0470 (DPF differential pressure sensor) or similar in diagnostic scanner

## Tools needed

- Diagnostic scanner (MUT-III or equivalent) to read DPF soot load percentage (optional but helpful)
- Gas station pump access to diesel fuel

## Tips

- Some owners report that towing (loaded trailer) helps trigger regen due to sustained high load and engine heat. If you frequently tow, DPF issues are less common
- Fuel quality matters. Low-quality diesel with high sulfur content loads the DPF faster. Use premium/branded fuel (Shell, Caltex, major chains)
- If the warning light comes on after every few days of city driving, the vehicle is not accumulating enough highway time for regen. Increase highway driving or consider a long trip to reset the system
- Never disable the DPF or remove it (illegal in most countries and voids emissions compliance). Modern engines are tuned for DPF back-pressure; removing it causes ECU relearning issues and potential damage
- If your vehicle is in limp-mode (reduced power) due to DPF load, this is intentional — the ECU reduces soot production to prevent catastrophic overloading. Do not try to "clear" the light or force more power; it will only make the situation worse

Solenoid-operated EGR valve, intake manifold mounted
Only replace if valve is seized or fails actuation test after cleaning. $150–350. GenOEM EV113GEN is a compatible aftermarket equivalent.
Replace every time the valve is removed. $5–15.

Replace if disturbing the cooler pipes. $5–15 each.
Diesel intake cleaner spray (e.g., Liqui-Moly Diesel Intake Cleaner)

The 4M41 3.2 DI-D diesel recirculates exhaust gas through the intake manifold to reduce NOx emissions. Over time — typically 80,000–120,000 km — soot and carbon deposits coat the EGR valve, EGR cooler, and intake manifold, restricting airflow. Symptoms are gradual power loss, black smoke, and rough idle. Unlike a failed EGR valve (which triggers a fault code), a dirty EGR is a maintenance issue with no warning light. Cleaning restores fuel economy and power without replacing parts. The EGR sits at the rear-right of the engine under the plastic cover, secured to the intake manifold with four bolts.

## Symptoms

- Gradual power loss over months with no fault code
- Black smoke from exhaust, especially under hard acceleration
- Rough or lumpy idle that has worsened over time
- Increased fuel consumption with no obvious cause
- Check Engine light with EGR-related codes (P0400 series)
- Sticky or non-responsive EGR valve on actuation test with scan tool

## Tools needed

- Phillips screwdriver (engine cover removal)
- 10mm socket (EGR valve bolts, engine cover)
- 12mm socket and ring spanner (EGR pipe bolts)
- 2" extension bar
- Wire brush and plastic scraper (carbon removal)
- Rags and nitrile gloves (carbon is carcinogenic — wear gloves)

## Tips

- The EGR valve is at the rear-right of the 4M41 engine when viewed from the front, mounted on top of the intake manifold. Remove the plastic engine cover (2–3 bolts) to access it.
- Before removing the valve, disconnect the electrical connector and any coolant hoses to the EGR cooler. Have rags ready — residual coolant will drip from the cooler hoses.
- Carbon removal: use a wire brush and plastic scraper to remove deposits from the EGR valve seat, the valve port in the manifold, and the intake manifold runners. An old toothbrush works well in tight areas. Spray intake cleaner solvent to soften heavy deposits.
- Do not use compressed air to blow carbon into the manifold — dislodged carbon particles can enter cylinders and score bores. Wipe out all loose carbon before reinstalling.
- Test the cleaned EGR valve before reinstalling: connect 12V to the solenoid terminals — the valve should open and close cleanly. If it sticks or does not fully open, it needs replacement.
- The intake manifold itself accumulates carbon on the swirl flap ports and manifold walls. If accessible without full manifold removal, clean these areas at the same time. Significant carbon buildup in the manifold usually requires manifold removal for proper cleaning.
- After reassembly, clear any stored EGR fault codes with an OBD-II scanner and perform a test drive including full-throttle acceleration to verify the cleaning has restored performance.
- Service interval: Mitsubishi does not publish a cleaning interval, but 80,000–100,000 km is the community-standard first clean on vehicles used mainly in stop-start city driving. Highway-driven vehicles accumulate deposits more slowly.

Engine oil is the lifeblood of your engine. Choosing the right grade, type, and capacity keeps the 4M41 diesel and 6G75 petrol running smoothly, protects against wear, and extends engine life. Both engines use the same oil grades but have different capacities. This guide covers oil selection, capacity, and change intervals for your Pajero IV.

## Tips

- Always check the oil level when cold (before starting the engine) using the dipstick. Top up if below the minimum mark.
- Change both oil AND filter together at each service interval. A clogged filter reduces oil flow and damages the engine.
- Never mix oil types or viscosities. Stick to the recommended grade for your climate.
- Diesel engines (4M41) produce more ash and sludge than petrol — the 15,000 km interval is firm. Shorter intervals (10,000 km) are recommended for severe driving.
- Petrol engines (6G75) are more tolerant of slightly extended intervals, but 15,000 km is the factory recommendation.
- Synthetic oils (5W-30) last longer than mineral oils. Most modern Pajero IVs run well on synthetics.
- Using the wrong oil grade or type can void your warranty and cause engine damage.

An engine that refuses to start can have dozens of root causes. This guide walks you through the most common failure modes for the 4M41 diesel and 6G75 petrol, from simple (dead battery) to complex (failed fuel pump, bad starter). We focus on systematic diagnosis rather than guessing, to isolate the problem quickly without unnecessary parts replacement.

## Symptoms

- Key turn produces no crank (silent, no starter noise) — likely battery or electrical
- Key turn produces slow crank (sluggish starter) — weak battery or bad starter
- Engine cranks normally but does not fire (no ignition) — fuel or ignition issue
- Engine fires but dies immediately (backfires, rough idle) — ignition timing or fuel quality

## Tools needed

- Multimeter or analog voltmeter (to measure battery voltage, fuel pump signal)
- Jumper cables (to test if weak battery is the issue)
- Socket set and screwdrivers (to disconnect battery terminals, remove spark plugs, etc.)
- Flashlight or headlamp (work under the car, see in dark engine bay)
- Spark plug socket and ratchet (petrol engines, to check for spark)
- MUT-III scan tool (optional; to read diagnostic codes if Check Engine light is on)

## Tips

- Do NOT crank the engine continuously for more than 10 seconds at a time. This drains the battery and overheats the starter. Crank for 5 seconds, wait 30 seconds, try again
- A completely dead battery (0V) cannot be jump-started. It must be charged or replaced. Even a weak battery (10V) can sometimes crank slowly and might eventually catch
- If the engine cranks but won't fire, DO NOT keep cranking — you are wasting fuel and battery. Stop, diagnose, then try again
- Diesel engines are harder to start cold than petrol engines. The glow plug warm-up is essential. If you skip this step (turn key straight to START without waiting 3–5 seconds for glow plug preheat), cold starts may fail
- On very cold mornings (below −10°C), diesel fuel can gel, preventing fuel flow. If fuel looks cloudy, add diesel fuel additive (Techron, Power Service) or drain/replace the tank
- Petrol engines with flooded cylinders (too much fuel, not enough spark) should be cranked with the throttle wide open to lean the mixture and allow air in
- If the engine turns over weakly but catches and idles, suspect a weak battery — charge it fully before attempting to drive

Lighting faults on the Pajero IV usually follow a simple pattern: a single light out means a bulb; multiple lights on one circuit means a fuse or relay; all lights dim or flicker means a poor earth or charging fault. The headlamp auto-levelling system (where fitted) has its own ECU and can trigger the warning lamp on the dash. Work through the fuse box before replacing bulbs or switches.

## Symptoms

- One headlight out — check bulb first, then check for moisture in housing
- Both headlights out — check HEADLAMP fuse and relay in engine bay box
- Tail lights and dash lights out together — same fuse circuit (TAIL or ILLUM)
- One indicator not flashing (hyper-flash on other side) — failed bulb in that corner
- All indicators not working — check HAZARD fuse and hazard/flasher relay
- Reversing light not working — check bulb, then reverse gear switch on gearbox
- Brake lights not working — check bulb, then brake light switch above brake pedal
- Fog lights not working — check FOG fuse and fog lamp switch continuity

## Tips

- Use the fuse box lid label as your first reference — every circuit is listed. The Pajero IV has two fuse boxes: engine bay (high-current circuits) and interior/dash (lower-current circuits under the steering column).
- Tail lights and instrument illumination share a fuse — if both go out at once, check the TAIL fuse in the interior fuse box before suspecting a switch.
- Brake light failure: the most common cause is the brake light switch at the top of the brake pedal. Press the brake pedal by hand with the ignition on and check for 12V at the switch output terminal. The switch is easily replaced — it unplugs and screws in.
- Reversing light failure: if the bulb is good, check the reverse switch on the side of the gearbox or transmission. On the automatic, the inhibitor switch (park/neutral switch) controls the reverse light signal.
- Headlamp levelling fault warning (dashboard): the auto-levelling ECU monitors the level sensor and levelling motors. A fault code can be read with an OBD-II scanner supporting enhanced Mitsubishi protocols. The most common cause is a failed levelling motor or a corroded sensor connector at the front suspension.
- Moisture in headlamp housings: the Pajero IV headlamp seals age and admit moisture. Condensation inside the lens is common. Remove the dust cap at the rear of the housing and allow it to ventilate before sealing with new weatherstrip — replacing the whole housing is rarely necessary.
- Earth connections: if multiple lights in a zone are dim or intermittent, trace the earth wire back to the body earthing point. Rear lamp earths on the Pajero IV are prone to corrosion at the tailgate body stud.
