P0087 — Fuel Rail Pressure Too Low
First thing to replace for P0087. $30–60. Some early 2008 use 1770A021.

P0087 means the ECU detected fuel rail pressure below the commanded target. On the 4M41 common-rail diesel, this is a serious code — insufficient fuel pressure causes hard starting, loss of power, and can leave you stranded. Most common causes are a clogged fuel filter, weak high-pressure fuel pump, or a leaking high-pressure injector. Address immediately.

## Symptoms

- Hard to start or long cranking before firing
- Loss of power under load, especially above 2000 RPM
- Black or white smoke from exhaust
- Rough idle or engine cutting out
- Check Engine light illuminated
- DPF regeneration failing (insufficient fuel pressure)

## Tips

- Always replace the fuel filter before diagnosing further — it is cheap, takes 30 minutes, and fixes this code in 40% of cases.
- Do not run a common-rail diesel on low fuel — the fuel itself lubricates the high-pressure pump. Repeated low-fuel operation accelerates pump wear.
- Air in the fuel system causes the same symptoms. After filter replacement, prime the system by cycling the ignition 3–4 times before cranking.
- If you've had contaminated fuel (water or wrong fuel type), drain the tank and flush the entire system before replacing the filter — the contamination will destroy the new filter quickly.

P0100 — Mass Air Flow (MAF) Circuit Malfunction

Code P0100 indicates the engine control unit (ECU) has detected a problem with the Mass Air Flow (MAF) sensor circuit. The MAF sensor measures incoming air volume and helps the ECU calculate the correct fuel mixture. A faulty MAF sensor causes poor fuel economy, hesitation, and rough idle. This code is specific to petrol engines (6G75); diesel engines (4M41) do not have MAF sensors.

## Symptoms

- Check Engine light illuminated
- Rough idle (engine shakes at stoplight)
- Hesitation when accelerating
- Reduced fuel economy (10-20% worse)
- Engine stumbles or stalls at low RPM
- Black smoke from exhaust (overfueling)
- Loss of power under load

## Tips

- Always replace the engine air filter when diagnosed with P0100. A clogged filter commonly triggers this code.
- Do NOT use carburator cleaner or compressed air on the MAF sensor — this damages the sensitive heating element. Use only MAF-specific cleaner.
- MAF sensor contamination is often caused by a failing or clogged air filter. After cleaning the MAF sensor, replace the air filter to prevent recurrence.
- If the code returns after cleaning, the sensor is likely failing and needs replacement.
- A vacuum leak (cracked hose or loose connector) can also trigger this code by allowing unmeasured air into the engine. Inspect all air intake hoses.

P0128 means the engine is not reaching normal operating temperature within the expected time, or is running cooler than the thermostat's rated opening point. The most common cause by far is a stuck-open thermostat, which keeps coolant circulating through the radiator constantly and prevents the engine from warming up fully. Symptoms are subtle — heater that doesn't get hot, poor fuel economy, and a temperature gauge that never reaches the midpoint.

## Symptoms

- Check Engine light illuminated
- Heater not producing hot air (engine runs cold)
- Temperature gauge stays low or never reaches midpoint
- Poor fuel economy (cold engine runs richer)
- Slightly rough idle (fuel trims not optimised for cold engine)

## Tips

- A stuck-open thermostat is much more common than a stuck-closed one. The spring mechanism wears open, not shut.
- Thermostats are a wear item — replace every 100,000 km or when diagnosing a cold-running engine.
- Always drain enough coolant before removing the thermostat housing — usually 1.5–2 litres is enough to prevent coolant spillage.
- After replacing thermostat, run engine with heater on full and bleed air from the cooling system (look for air purge screw on thermostat housing).
- If you notice the temperature gauge sitting just below midpoint but the heater works well, the car is likely fine — check gauge calibration before replacing parts.

P0171 means the ECU's long-term fuel trim is adding more than ~10% extra fuel to compensate for a lean air-fuel mixture. The engine is getting too much air or too little fuel relative to what the MAF sensor expects. Common causes include vacuum leaks (unmeasured air), a dirty MAF sensor, or a weak fuel pump. This is a petrol-only code — the 4M41 diesel does not use the same fuel trim system.

- Check Engine light illuminated
- Rough idle or hunting idle (RPM fluctuating)
- Hesitation on acceleration from low RPM
- Slightly poor fuel economy
- Engine may stall at idle when A/C is switched on

## Tips

- Start with a vacuum leak check — they are free to diagnose and cheap to fix. A cracked PCV hose is the most commonly missed cause.
- MAF sensor cleaning is a 10-minute job and worth trying before replacing the sensor.
- Do NOT use carburettor cleaner on the MAF sensor — it damages the hot-wire element. Use only MAF-specific spray.
- If P0171 appears alongside P0174 (Bank 2 lean), the cause is almost certainly a large vacuum leak or a bad MAF — both banks can't coincidentally run lean independently.
- A code that appears only after hard acceleration may indicate a weak fuel pump unable to maintain pressure under demand.

## Factory procedure reference

- **Factory procedure — Engine Control (Section 80)** (M1, section 80)

Code P0300 indicates the ECU has detected random misfires across multiple cylinders. A misfire occurs when one or more cylinders fail to ignite the fuel-air mixture, causing power loss, rough idle, and hesitation. Unlike P0301-P0306 (which point to a specific cylinder), P0300 means the problem affects multiple cylinders randomly. Common causes are weak spark, fuel delivery issues, or air leaks.

## Symptoms

- Check Engine light illuminated
- Rough idle (noticeable vibration)
- Hesitation or stumbling during acceleration
- Loss of power, especially under load
- Black smoke from exhaust (overfueling)
- Fuel smell from exhaust
- Poor fuel economy
- Engine may run and then stall

## Tips

- Start with spark plug replacement — it's the most common cause and cheapest fix.
- If P0300 appears after refueling at an unfamiliar station, suspect fuel contamination. Drain tank and refill with quality fuel from a trusted brand.
- Vacuum leaks (cracked hoses, loose fittings) are the second most common cause. Spray soapy water to find them — bubbles reveal the leak.
- Do NOT ignore this code. Continued driving with misfires damages the catalytic converter (very expensive).
- If misfire codes appear only on cold mornings, suspect failing glow plugs (4M41 diesel) or old battery (weak cold start).
- Water in fuel is common in humid climates or after driving through flooded roads. A fuel dryer additive may help; if not, drain tank.

## Factory procedure reference

- **Factory procedure — Engine Control (Section 80)** (M1, section 80)
P0335 — Crankshaft Position Sensor Circuit Malfunction
Compatible with both engines. Verify against VIN at dealer. $50–120.

P0335 means the ECU is not receiving a valid signal from the crankshaft position sensor (CKP). The CKP tells the ECU the exact rotational position and speed of the crankshaft, which is essential for calculating ignition timing and fuel injection. Without it, the engine will not start or will stall immediately. This is a critical code — the vehicle will typically not run.

## Symptoms

- Engine cranks but will not start (most common)
- Engine starts then immediately stalls
- Check Engine light illuminated
- No-start condition with no other obvious cause
- Intermittent stalling at speed (sensor intermittently losing signal)

## Tips

- P0335 almost always means a no-start. If the engine is running and this code appears, it is likely intermittent — diagnose the wiring first.
- The CKP sensor is a wear item on high-mileage vehicles. Replace it proactively if other sensors on the harness are showing age.
- Wiring harness damage near the crankshaft pulley is common — the lower engine gets heat cycling and vibration that degrades insulation.
- After replacement, clear the code and test-drive over 20 km including highway speeds before confirming the repair.

## Factory procedure reference

- **Factory procedure — Engine Control (Section 80)** (M1, section 80)
P0340 — Camshaft Position Sensor Circuit Malfunction
6G75 has MIVEC on intake cam. Exhaust cam sensor part may differ. $60–150.

P0340 indicates the ECU is not receiving a valid signal from the camshaft position sensor (CMP). The CMP tells the ECU the position of the camshaft relative to the crankshaft, enabling correct injection timing and variable valve timing (MIVEC on 6G75). A failed CMP sensor can cause hard starting, rough running, or a no-start condition. Unlike P0335 (crankshaft), the engine can sometimes run poorly with a failed camshaft sensor.

## Symptoms

- Check Engine light illuminated
- Hard starting or extended cranking before start
- Rough idle or fluctuating RPM
- Reduced power and hesitation
- Engine may start but run poorly
- Fault codes for MIVEC/VVT system appearing alongside

## Tips

- P0340 + P0335 together is a red flag — suspect a jumped timing belt before replacing sensors.
- The CMP sensor is often located near oil contamination zones on the cylinder head. Always inspect the connector for oil intrusion.
- On the 6G75 with MIVEC, low oil level or old, degraded oil can trigger camshaft-related codes without the sensor itself being faulty. Check oil first.
- After replacement, test-drive and monitor with scanner to confirm CAM signal is tracking correctly with CRK signal.

## Factory procedure reference

- **Factory procedure — Engine Control (Section 80)** (M1, section 80)
P0380 — Glow Plug / Heater Circuit A Malfunction
OEM part. Buy a set of 4. $15–30 each. NGK Y-508J and DENSO DG-114 are equivalent.
Check this if all glow plugs test good. $30–80.

P0380 indicates a fault in the glow plug pre-heat circuit on the 4M41 diesel engine. Glow plugs heat the combustion chamber to aid cold starting — without them, a diesel engine is very difficult to start below 5°C. The fault may be in the glow plug relay, the glow plug controller module, the wiring to the plugs, or one or more failed glow plugs. This is a diesel-only code.

## Symptoms

- Check Engine light illuminated
- Difficult cold starting (especially below 5°C)
- White smoke on startup (unburned diesel due to cold combustion)
- Rough idle until engine warms up
- Glow plug light on dashboard stays on longer than normal or does not illuminate

## Tips

- Replace all 4 glow plugs as a set even if only one has failed. The others are the same age and will follow shortly.
- Glow plugs are prone to seizing in the cylinder head after years of heat cycling. Apply penetrating oil (WD-40 or Plus Gas) and let it soak for 30 minutes before removal.
- If a glow plug snaps during removal, the remaining stub must be drilled and extracted — a workshop job.
- The glow plug relay is often mounted in the engine bay fuse/relay box. It is cheap and worth replacing if you cannot identify which plug is failing.
- Glow plugs do not affect warm starting — if the car starts fine when warm but struggles in cold mornings, P0380 is the likely culprit.

## Factory procedure reference

- **Factory procedure — Glow Plug System (Section 16)** (M1, section 16)
P0400 — Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) Flow Malfunction

Code P0400 indicates the EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation) system is not flowing exhaust gas back into the intake as intended. The EGR system reduces emissions by recirculating a small amount of exhaust gas, which reduces combustion temperature and harmful pollutants. A stuck or blocked EGR valve, failed EGR solenoid, or carbon buildup can trigger this code. This is a complex diagnosis requiring careful testing.

## Symptoms

- Check Engine light illuminated
- Rough idle
- Hesitation or stumbling during acceleration
- Loss of power under load
- Ping or knock sound during acceleration (EGR system failing to cool combustion)
- Slightly higher exhaust temperature
- Fault may be intermittent on first startup

## Tips

- EGR problems are common on high-mileage vehicles (100,000+ km) due to carbon accumulation.
- A stuck EGR valve can sometimes be freed by running a fuel system cleaner additive through the tank; not always effective but cheap to try first.
- Do NOT attempt to remove the EGR valve without draining coolant first — the cooler is integrated with the cooling system.
- If code returns after repair, carbon may be building up again. Quality fuel and regular fuel injector cleaning help prevent recurrence.
- EGR problems can cause pinging during acceleration — do NOT ignore this as it can damage the engine if prolonged.
- Some older Pajeros have a history of EGR solenoid failures. If replacement fixes the code, monitor for recurrence at future services.

## Factory procedure reference

- **Factory procedure — Emission Control (Section 14)** (M1, section 14)
P0420 — Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1)
Check this before replacing the catalytic converter. $80–180.
Upstream sensor — may need replacement if downstream test reveals upstream fault. $80–180.

P0420 means the downstream oxygen sensor is reading an exhaust pattern too similar to the upstream sensor, indicating the catalytic converter is not converting pollutants efficiently. The catalytic converter typically lasts 150,000–200,000 km but degrades with oil burning, lead fuel, and severe misfires. Before replacing the expensive cat, confirm the oxygen sensors are not the cause of the false reading. This is a petrol-only code.

## Symptoms

- Check Engine light illuminated
- No noticeable drivability symptoms in early stages
- Slight sulphur (rotten egg) smell from exhaust
- Failed emissions test
- May appear alongside P0300 (misfire) codes which accelerate catalyst damage

## Tips

- Always replace the oxygen sensor before the catalytic converter — a $150 O2 sensor fix is much better than a $1200 cat.
- P0420 after spark plug replacement or misfire repair is common — misfires send raw fuel into the cat, which can melt the ceramic substrate.
- Aftermarket catalytic converters are significantly cheaper than OEM ($200–500 vs $800–1200), but may not last as long.
- Engine oil leaking past valve stem seals or piston rings will destroy even a new catalytic converter. Fix the oil consumption first.
- P0420 alone does not affect drivability — you can drive safely while diagnosing, but the vehicle will fail an emissions test.

## Factory procedure reference

- **Factory procedure — Emission Control (Section 14)** (M1, section 14)
P0440 — Evaporative Emission Control System Malfunction
Fuel filler cap (first thing to check)
Locking filler cap with pressure relief
Check this first. $15–40. Non-locking version also available.
$40–100. Located in engine bay on the intake side.

P0440 indicates a large leak or malfunction in the EVAP (evaporative emission control) system. This system captures fuel vapour from the fuel tank and routes it into the intake manifold to be burned rather than venting to atmosphere. The most common cause is a loose or damaged fuel filler cap — always start there. This is a petrol-only code; the 4M41 diesel does not have an EVAP system.

## Symptoms

- Check Engine light illuminated
- Smell of fuel from outside the vehicle (fuel vapour escaping)
- No drivability symptoms in most cases
- Failed emissions test

## Tips

- Always start with the fuel filler cap — it is free to inspect and $15–40 to replace. It fixes P0440 in 40% of cases.
- Tighten the cap until it clicks. Many drivers tighten it too loosely, causing intermittent P0440.
- If the fuel smell is strong and persistent, suspect a cracked canister or disconnected hose — this is also a fire hazard and should be fixed promptly.
- P0440 does not affect engine performance. The vehicle is safe to drive while diagnosing, but should not be ignored due to emissions and fire risk.

P0500 indicates the ECU is not receiving a valid signal from the vehicle speed sensor (VSS). The VSS provides road speed data to the ECU, ABS module, and speedometer. Without it, the speedometer will read zero, ABS may activate unexpectedly, and the automatic transmission may not shift correctly. Usually caused by a failed sensor or damaged wiring.

## Symptoms

- Check Engine light illuminated
- Speedometer reads zero at all times
- Erratic speedometer behaviour (needle bouncing)
- Automatic transmission staying in low gears or shifting erratically
- ABS light or traction control light also illuminated
- Idle speed higher than normal (ECU using air/fuel map that ignores vehicle speed)

## Tips

- The VSS is mounted in an exposed location under the vehicle and is prone to connector corrosion. Inspect the connector carefully before condemning the sensor.
- If the ABS light is also on alongside P0500, the ABS wheel speed sensors may be providing the speed signal — check those as well.
- A faulty VSS can cause the automatic transmission to hunt between gears at low speeds. This is often misdiagnosed as a transmission fault.
- After replacement, a road test is essential to confirm the speedometer reads correctly and the code does not return.

## Factory procedure reference

- **Factory procedure — Speed Sensors (Section 35)** (M1, section 35)

P0700 is a generic alert code meaning the Transmission Control Module (TCM) has detected a fault and stored its own fault code. P0700 itself does not describe the specific fault — it is the TCM signalling the ECU that there is a transmission problem. You must read the TCM fault codes separately using an OBD-II scanner that supports enhanced (manufacturer-specific) codes. Common companion codes include P0715 (input speed sensor), P0720 (output speed sensor), and P0750–P0770 (solenoid faults).

## Symptoms

- Check Engine light illuminated
- Transmission warning light (if equipped)
- Harsh or erratic gear shifts
- Transmission stuck in single gear (limp mode)
- Delayed engagement when selecting Drive or Reverse
- Slipping between gears

## Tips

- P0700 alone tells you nothing specific — you must read the TCM's own codes with a scanner that supports Mitsubishi enhanced codes (not just generic OBD-II).
- Transmission fluid degradation is the most common cause. If fluid is dark brown or smells burnt, a full fluid and filter change may resolve the issue.
- Limp mode (stuck in 3rd gear) is the TCM protecting the transmission from further damage. Do not ignore it.
- A basic OBD-II scanner may show P0700 but not the specific sub-codes. A Mitsubishi-compatible scanner (or Snap-on/Autel with enhanced support) is needed.
- If the transmission worked perfectly before a fluid change and now has issues, the wrong fluid type was likely used. ATF SP-III is required — not generic Dexron III.
P0715 — Transmission Input / Turbine Speed Sensor Circuit
Replace whenever dropping the pan. Wrong fluid causes solenoid and sensor damage.

P0715 indicates the Transmission Control Module (TCM) is not receiving a valid signal from the input shaft (turbine) speed sensor inside the automatic transmission. This sensor measures how fast the torque converter turbine is spinning, which the TCM uses to calculate slip and control shift timing. A failed sensor causes erratic or harsh shifting and often triggers limp mode. Usually appears alongside P0700.

## Symptoms

- Check Engine light illuminated
- Harsh, erratic, or delayed gear shifts
- Transmission stuck in 3rd gear (limp mode)
- P0700 (TCM fault) also stored
- Speedometer may be unaffected (uses separate VSS)

## Tips

- Metal shavings on the sensor tip are a warning sign of internal transmission wear. Address the root cause or you will replace the sensor again soon.
- Always replace the transmission fluid and filter when dropping the pan to access the sensor — the labour cost is already paid.
- P0715 + P0720 (output speed sensor) together may indicate a wiring harness issue inside the pan rather than two failed sensors.
P2002 — Diesel Particulate Filter Efficiency Below Threshold

P2002 means the ECU has detected that the Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF) is not performing regeneration correctly, or the DPF is so clogged that it cannot be regenerated. The DPF traps soot particles and periodically burns them off during highway driving (passive regeneration) or via an active injection cycle. This code commonly appears on vehicles used predominantly for short urban trips, which prevent the exhaust from reaching regeneration temperature.

## Symptoms

- DPF warning light illuminated (amber lamp)
- Check Engine light illuminated
- Noticeable loss of power (limp mode in severe cases)
- Increased fuel consumption
- Oily residue from exhaust tip
- White or grey smoke at startup or under load

## Tips

- Use only ACEA C3 rated engine oil in the 4M41 with DPF. Standard ACEA A3/B4 oil contains higher ash content that cannot be burned off and permanently blocks the DPF.
- At least one monthly motorway drive at 60+ km/h for 30+ minutes is essential for DPF health. Urban-only driving will block the DPF within 50,000 km.
- Check engine oil level if P2002 appears — rising oil level is a sign of post-injection fuel contamination, which damages the engine and DPF simultaneously.
- Do NOT attempt to remove or bypass the DPF — this is illegal in most markets and triggers additional fault codes affecting engine management.
- If the vehicle is exclusively used in urban driving, consider professional DPF cleaning every 60,000–80,000 km as preventive maintenance.
- A forced regeneration should not be performed in an enclosed space — the exhaust temperature rises to 600°C+ during active regen.

Replace oil filter every 10,000 km, air filters every 20,000 km (more often in dusty conditions).

- Dirty cabin filter reduces AC cooling efficiency noticeably. If the fan is loud but airflow is weak, the cabin filter is likely blocked.

- Engine oil: always fill to MIN mark on dipstick, run engine 30 seconds, then recheck. Never trust volume alone.

The fuel filter on the 4M41 diesel engine protects the injection pump and injectors from contamination. Clogged filters increase fuel pressure, reduce efficiency, and in severe cases can trigger "Check Engine" warnings. Factory service intervals call for replacement every 60,000 km or annually. Diesel is hygroscopic — water absorbs into old filters, promoting microbial growth and corrosion in fuel systems.

## Symptoms

- Scheduled maintenance interval (60,000 km)
- Hard starting, especially in cold weather
- Loss of power or sluggish acceleration
- Check Engine light (P1276 - fuel filter pressure switch code)
- Increased fuel consumption or excessive fuel odor
- Visible water or discoloration in fuel bowl sight glass

## Tools needed

- Wrench or socket for fuel filter bowl (usually 75mm hex or 3/8" drive socket)
- Pan or drain container for residual fuel
- Clean shop towels or compressed air
- New fuel line clamps (optional, if original clamps are loose)
- Fuel system priming tool or hand pump (for air bleeding on some models)

## Tips

- The fuel filter cartridge is located on the driver's side of the engine bay, mounted vertically on the chassis frame.
- Before loosening, place a drain pan underneath — fuel will spill when you remove the bowl.
- Keep the engine off and key out of the ignition for at least 10 minutes before working on fuel lines. Diesel engines can hold significant pressure even when off.
- After replacing the element, the system contains air. Start the engine and let it idle for 30–60 seconds to self-bleed; you may hear a slight knock or rattling as air purges through the injector pump.
- If the engine does not start or runs very rough after replacement, perform manual priming via the fuel pump hand primer (small rubber bulb on some models) or use the MUT-III scan tool to command fuel pump prime cycles.
- Dispose of old fuel filters as hazardous waste — they contain trapped diesel, which is flammable and toxic.
- Some owners extend intervals to 80,000 km in clean environments; reduce to 40,000 km if towing or driving dusty/unpaved roads.

Fuse box locations and fuse assignments for the 2008–2013 Pajero IV. There are two fuse boxes: the engine bay fuse/relay box (left side of engine bay) and the interior fuse box (behind the glovebox panel, passenger side). Always replace a blown fuse with the identical amperage rating — a fuse that keeps blowing indicates an electrical fault, not a weak fuse.

## Symptoms

- Electrical component stopped working suddenly (lights, windows, wipers, accessories)
- Check Engine or warning light on for an electrical system
- Intermittent fault that clears itself (borderline-blown fuse)

## Tips

- The engine bay fuse box cover has a fuse layout diagram printed on the inside of the lid — use it before consulting the manual.
- The interior fuse box is located behind the lower glovebox panel. Open the glovebox, press the side clips to drop it down, and the fuse box is above.
- A fuse that blows repeatedly means there is a short circuit downstream. Replacing with a higher-rated fuse is dangerous — do not do it.
- Check fuses visually: the thin metal bridge inside the fuse should be intact. Some fuses look fine visually but are blown internally — test with a multimeter if unsure.
- Tail light, interior light, and accessory socket faults are almost always a blown fuse before anything more complex.
- If a fuse blows immediately on replacement, unplug the load (lamp, accessory) first to confirm it is the fault source before replacing the fuse again.

Front brake pad replacement on the Gen 3 Pajero (NM / NP series, 2000–2006) is a straightforward job accessible to any home mechanic. The front brakes use a single sliding caliper on each side with two pads. Always replace pads in axle pairs (both fronts together) and inspect the disc surface for deep scoring or thickness below the minimum. The Gen 3 front caliper is similar to Gen 4 but with minor differences in caliper guide pin dimensions — use Gen 3 specific parts.

## Symptoms

- Squealing or grinding when braking
- Brake warning light (pad wear indicator)
- Reduced braking performance or longer stopping distance
- Brake pad thickness below 2 mm (replace at 3 mm or less)
- Vibration through the brake pedal (may indicate scored disc)

## Tools needed

- Jack and axle stands (or vehicle ramps)
- Wheel brace (to remove wheel bolts)
- 12 mm socket or spanner (caliper slide pin bolts)
- 17 mm or 19 mm spanner (wheel bolts)
- Brake caliper piston wind-back tool or large G-clamp
- Flat-blade screwdriver
- Wire brush (to clean caliper mounting bracket)
- Brake cleaner spray
- Torque wrench

## Tips

- Never press the brake pedal while the caliper is removed — the piston will shoot out.
- If the fluid reservoir is already at maximum, syringe some out before pushing the piston back — otherwise it will overflow and damage paint.
- If slide pins are seized (caliper slides on one pin only), the pad on one side will wear much faster than the other — always check both pins move freely.
- Bed-in (breaking-in) new pads is important — immediately doing hard stops with new pads can glaze the friction material.
- If the pads are at minimum and the discs are deeply grooved, replace both. Fitting new pads on scored discs reduces braking performance and causes accelerated pad wear.

Diesel engines require glow plugs to preheat the combustion chamber for cold starts. The 4M41 has four glow plugs, one per cylinder, controlled by the engine ECU. A failed glow plug causes hard cold starts, white smoke on startup, and rough idling until the engine warms. If all four glow plugs fail, the engine becomes nearly impossible to start below 5°C. Replacement is straightforward but requires care to avoid breaking the ceramic element inside the bore.

## Symptoms

- Difficulty starting in cold weather (below 10°C)
- White smoke on cold startup (incomplete combustion)
- Rough idle for 30+ seconds after a cold start
- Check Engine light with glow plug-related code (P0380, P0671–P0674)
- Hesitation or stumbling during warm-up phase
- One or more glow plug indicator lights not illuminating on the dashboard

## Tools needed

- Glow plug socket (usually 10mm or 12mm hex bit, with a rubber insert to protect ceramic element)
- Standard socket wrench and ratchet
- Spark plug gap tool or feeler gauge (to check plug burn condition if inspecting old plugs)
- Thermal jacket or insulated socket (recommended to avoid shocking the ceramic with temperature changes)
- Engine cover removal tools (plastic clips or screws, depending on year/model)

## Tips

- Glow plugs are located on top of the engine, under a plastic cover (labeled "ENGINE" or similar). Carefully remove plastic clips to expose them.
- The glow plug connectors are a single multi-pin connector block on the driver's side. You may need to unclip it to access the plugs, or work around it.
- If a plug breaks inside the bore during removal, stop and consult a professional. Broken ceramic can score the cylinder head and require machine work.
- Cold soak the vehicle overnight before replacement if possible — cold metal breaks less easily than warm. Do not remove glow plugs from a hot engine.
- After installing new plugs, allow the engine to idle for 2–3 minutes before driving. The ECU learns glow plug resistance on startup.
- Premature glow plug failure (before 80,000 km) often indicates a fuel injection timing issue or water in the fuel. If you replace plugs and they fail again quickly, have the injection pump checked.
- Some 2008–2009 models use MD319913 instead of MD319950. Always cross-reference your VIN with a dealer parts catalog.

The Pajero IV uses halogen headlights as standard, with HID (xenon) as an option on higher trim levels. Most bulbs can be replaced from behind the headlamp housing without removing it. HID headlights use a D2S discharge bulb — do not touch the burner with bare hands, and note that HID systems carry dangerous high voltage even with the ignition off. Indicators and tail/brake lights use standard bayonet bulbs accessible from inside the boot or behind trim panels.

## Symptoms

- Headlight not working on one side (single bulb or fuse failure)
- Both headlights out — check fuse or relay, not bulbs (rare for both to fail together)
- Indicator flashing faster than normal (hyper-flash) — bulb in that circuit has failed
- Reversing light not working — check bulb and reverse switch
- Fog light not working — separate fuse circuit

## Tools needed

- Plastic trim clip removal tool (for boot liner on tail light access)
- Gloves (for halogen bulb handling)

## Tips

- Never touch a halogen or HID bulb glass with bare fingers — skin oils cause hot spots that shorten bulb life dramatically. Use gloves or a clean cloth.
- HID headlights: the system stores charge for several minutes after power is removed. Disconnect the battery and wait 5 minutes before handling the ballast or igniter. The discharge voltage is lethal.
- Headlight access on the Pajero IV: reach in from behind the headlamp unit — there is a rubber dust cap over each bulb. Twist and remove the cap, then disconnect the wiring plug, twist the bulb retaining ring, and pull the bulb out.
- Indicator hyper-flash (fast flashing): the indicator relay senses circuit current — a blown bulb in the front or rear circuit reduces current and triggers fast flash. Find and replace the failed bulb before the rapid flash misleads you into replacing the relay.
- Tail light and brake light access: open the tailgate, fold back the boot liner at the rear corners, and remove the plastic nut fasteners to access the lamp cluster from inside.
- Front fog lights: accessible via a cutout in the front bumper — no bumper removal needed on most Pajero IV models. Reach in from below.
- If an indicator bulb is replaced but hyper-flash continues, check the earth connection at the lamp housing — poor earths cause false low-current readings at the flasher relay.

When the heater blows cold or lukewarm air, the cause is usually a stuck-open thermostat (engine never reaches temperature), a blocked heater core, or low coolant level. On the dual-zone climate system, a faulty blend door actuator can also prevent hot air from reaching the cabin even when the engine is warm. Diagnose in order — thermostat is cheapest and most common.

## Symptoms

- Heater blows cold or barely warm air even after engine reaches temperature
- Temperature gauge stays low (thermostat stuck open)
- Only one zone (front or rear) producing warm air
- Gurgling or bubbling sounds from dashboard (air in heater core)
- Sweet coolant smell inside cabin (heater core leak)
- Windows fogging excessively from inside (heater core weeping)

## Tips

- Feel the heater hoses (the two hoses running through the firewall) with the engine warm. Both should be hot. If the outlet hose is cold, the heater core is blocked.
- If the temperature gauge never reaches midpoint, the thermostat is stuck open — this prevents the engine reaching the temperature needed to heat the heater core.
- Check coolant level in the expansion tank first. Low coolant means the heater core may not be getting flow.
- Gurgling from the dashboard indicates air in the heater core. Bleed the cooling system by running the engine with the heater on max and the expansion tank cap removed until bubbles stop.
- A sweet smell inside the cabin or fogged windows with a film on the inside of the glass is almost always a leaking heater core. This requires dashboard removal — a major workshop job ($500–1200 labour).
- If only the rear zone is cold, check the rear blower motor — it is separate from the front unit and has its own fuse.
- Blend door actuator faults produce a clicking noise from behind the dashboard and incorrect temperature response. These are common on high-mileage vehicles and require dashboard access.

Regular maintenance is the most cost-effective way to keep your Pajero IV running reliably and avoid expensive repairs. Mitsubishi specifies service intervals every 10,000 km or 12 months (whichever comes first) for most items. This guide covers the complete maintenance schedule from first service (1,000 km) through major services at 100,000+ km.

## Tips

- Keep detailed service records in your logbook — they prove maintenance history if you sell the vehicle.
- Don't skip major services to save money. A $300 service now prevents a $3,000 repair later.
- If you exceed the service interval significantly (20%+ over), still service the vehicle and inform the technician of the overdue work.
- Synthetic oils last longer (8,000-10,000 km intervals possible), but use factory-recommended viscosity.
- Have your mechanic explain any 'additional work required' — don't blindly approve it. Get a second opinion if the cost seems high.
- Dealer service is more expensive but uses genuine parts and warranty-backed labor. Independent workshops are cheaper but quality varies.

Approximately 2.3 L (fill to filler plug level)
Confirm correct specification in your owner's handbook — do not use GL-5 without verification, as GL-5 additives can damage synchroniser rings. $15–25 per litre.
Replace every time the drain plug is removed. $2–5.

Manual transmission Pajero IV models use a separate gear oil for the gearbox, distinct from the transfer case fluid. The gearbox oil lubricates the gear sets, synchronisers, and bearings. It is not changed at regular intervals like engine oil — Mitsubishi treats it as a lifetime fill, but in practice the fluid degrades through heat cycling and moisture absorption, especially in vehicles used off-road or for towing. Inspection at 80,000 km and replacement every 80,000– 100,000 km is good preventive maintenance. Worn or contaminated fluid accelerates synchro wear and causes hard gear selection.

## Symptoms

- Routine service interval (80,000–100,000 km)
- Hard or notchy gear changes, especially when cold
- Metallic contamination or dark colour found when inspecting fluid
- Gear whine or noise during acceleration (indicating oil starvation)
- Difficulty engaging specific gears (synchro wear exacerbated by low or degraded fluid)

## Tools needed

- 17mm hex key / Allen socket (drain and filler plugs)
- Drain pan (minimum 3L)
- Torque wrench
- Hand-operated fluid pump (for refilling through the side filler hole)

## Tips

- Always confirm the filler plug can be removed before draining — if it is seized you will not be able to refill.
- The filler/level hole is on the side of the gearbox casing, typically at mid-height. Fill until fluid drips from the filler hole — this is the correct level. Do not estimate by volume.
- Manual gearbox fluid is different from the transfer case fluid. Both have separate drain and filler plugs. The gearbox is forward in the drivetrain; the transfer case is behind it. Do not confuse the two.
- GL-4 and GL-5 are NOT interchangeable. GL-5 additives (extreme pressure sulphur compounds) corrode yellow metal alloys used in synchroniser rings on many manual gearboxes. Use GL-4 unless the manual specifies GL-5 or GL-4/5 dual-rated fluid.
- If the drained fluid is dark, smells burnt, or contains metallic particles, inspect the drain plug magnet carefully. Fine metallic fuzz is normal wear; larger flakes indicate accelerated component wear and the gearbox should be inspected before assuming fluid replacement alone will fix it.
- After an off-road water crossing (especially where the gearbox was submerged), change the fluid as soon as possible. Water contamination destroys synchronisers rapidly — water-contaminated gear oil looks milky or has a grey tint.

Manual Transmission Problems — Hard Shifting & Noise
Lip-type oil seal at gearbox to propshaft flange
Replace if oil is leaking at the rear of the gearbox. Can be done in-situ with the driveshaft removed. $15–35.
Replace if collapsed or cracked. $40–80. Simple bolt-on replacement.

The Pajero IV manual transmission is a robust unit but is subject to wear on synchroniser rings, selector forks, and bearings over high mileage. The most common complaints are hard or crunching gear changes (worn synchros), jumping out of gear under load (worn detents or selector forks), and gear noise at speed (bearing wear). Most of these faults develop progressively — early diagnosis and correct fluid maintenance prevents expensive overhaul. The transfer case (separate unit behind the gearbox) can produce similar symptoms but is diagnosed separately.

## Symptoms

- Crunching or grinding when selecting a gear (worn synchroniser on that gear)
- Hard to engage first or reverse gear, especially when stationary (clutch drag or synchro)
- Gear jumps out of engagement under load or on overrun (worn selector detent or fork)
- Whining or growling noise in all gears that changes with road speed (input/output bearing)
- Noise in neutral with engine running that disappears in gear (layshaft/countershaft bearing)
- Vibration through the gear lever (worn gearbox mounts or shift linkage)
- Oil leak at gearbox casing seams or output shaft seal

## Tools needed

- Multimeter or scan tool (to check clutch switch signal if engagement is difficult)
- Torque wrench
- 17mm Allen socket (drain and fill plugs)

## Tips

- Isolate gearbox from transfer case noise: the gearbox noise changes with road speed in all drive gears. Transfer case noise typically only appears in 4WD modes or changes between 2H and 4H.
- Crunching when changing gear is almost always synchroniser wear. If it only crunches on one specific gear (e.g., always on 3rd), that synchro is worn and the crunching will worsen over time. Changing to fresh GL-4 fluid may temporarily improve the problem but will not fix a worn synchro.
- Hard engagement of first or reverse from rest, with the clutch fully depressed: the most common cause is clutch drag (clutch not fully releasing). Check clutch pedal free play and hydraulic system before suspecting the gearbox.
- Gear jumping out under load: engage that gear, hold the stick in position by hand while driving, and confirm the noise or jump disappears. If it does, the selector detent spring is weak or the fork is worn. This is a workshop job requiring gearbox removal.
- Whine in neutral with engine running, disappearing in gear: this is the input shaft bearing or layshaft running unloaded. It is not urgent initially but indicates bearing wear that will progress. Monitor the oil for metallic contamination.
- Check gearbox mounts: vibration through the gear lever is often worn rubber engine or gearbox mounts transmitting vibration into the driveline, not internal gearbox damage. Inspect mounts for splitting or collapse before investigating internally.
- Output shaft seal leak: identify which seal is leaking by cleaning the area and noting where fresh oil appears. The rear output shaft seal is the most accessible and is replaceable without removing the gearbox.

The most important routine maintenance on any Pajero IV. Fresh oil protects the engine, reduces wear, and maintains fuel economy. Mitsubishi recommends every 10,000 km or 12 months — whichever comes first. On the 4M41 diesel, use only ACEA C3-rated oil if your vehicle has a DPF (diesel particulate filter).

## Symptoms

- Routine maintenance interval reached (10,000 km / 12 months)
- Oil level low on dipstick
- Oil appears dark brown or black on dipstick
- Oil pressure warning light illuminated

## Tools needed

- Oil drain pan (8L+ capacity)
- 17mm socket or ring spanner (drain plug)
- Oil filter removal strap wrench
- Funnel
- Torque wrench

## Tips

- Run the engine for 2–3 minutes before draining. Warm oil flows faster and carries more debris.
- 4M41 capacity: 6.5L without filter, 7.0L with new filter. Always check dipstick and top up accordingly.
- Pre-fill the new oil filter with fresh oil before fitting — reduces dry-start duration.
- Let the car sit on level ground for 5 minutes after filling before reading the dipstick.
- Dispose of used oil responsibly — most auto parts stores accept it.

Oil leaks are one of the most common complaints on older Pajero models. While some leaks are minor (slow seepage), others indicate serious problems (blown gaskets, cracked block). The key is identifying the leak source quickly before oil starvation causes engine damage. Most leaks are from worn gaskets or seals and are manageable repairs; a few require professional intervention.

## Symptoms

- Oil spots or puddles under the vehicle (check color and location)
- Burning oil smell from the engine bay or exhaust
- Low oil level on dipstick despite recent top-up
- Visible oil residue on engine block, valve cover, or pan
- Black or brown discoloration on engine exterior (accumulated oil)
- Smoke from the engine during acceleration (oil dripping on hot surfaces)

## Tools needed

- Gasket scraper or plastic scraper to remove old gasket material
- Socket set and screwdrivers for bolt removal
- Torque wrench for reinstallation
- Flashlight to inspect source
- Jack and jack stands (if accessing pan or underside)
- Clean rags and shop towels

## Tips

- Do not ignore small leaks. A slow 5 mL/day leak becomes 150 mL/month and can starve the engine of oil
- Always replace gaskets, never reuse. Old gaskets are compressed and lose sealing ability
- When removing a valve cover or pan, support the engine with a block of wood or engine hoist to avoid straining adjacent components
- Apply a thin bead of gasket maker (RTV silicone) on both sides of the new gasket for a better seal
- Tighten gasket bolts in a cross pattern (alternating opposite sides) to ensure even seating
- After repair, monitor oil level for 500 km to confirm the leak has stopped
- If a leak returns within weeks of repair, the gasket may not have seated properly or bolts may have loosened; re-torque and inspect

The Pajero IV outside mirrors have electric adjustment, optional heating, and optional electric folding. On vehicles with the ETACS (Electronic Time & Alarm Control System), the mirrors can be set to auto-fold when locking and unfold when unlocking. Mirror problems are usually electrical — a failed motor, a corroded connector in the door, or a blown fuse. The mirror adjustment switch is on the driver door trim; the fold switch (if fitted) is on the dash.

## Symptoms

- Mirror does not adjust in one or more directions — switch or motor fault
- Mirror does not fold or unfold electrically — fold motor or wiring
- Mirror heating not working in winter — check MIRROR HEAT fuse and heated mirror element
- Mirror position resets after folding — normal on ETACS vehicles (memorised to last switch position)
- Mirror glass cracked or fallen out — glass and motor/heater element replaceable separately

## Tools needed

- Trim removal tool (door card)
- Multimeter

## Tips

- Check the mirror fuse before anything else — the mirror circuit is typically labelled MIRROR or PWR MIRROR in the interior fuse box.
- Mirror heater fuse: separate to the adjustment motor circuit on most Pajero IV models. Labelled MIRROR HTR or DEFOG MIRROR.
- The door wiring harness passes through a flexible conduit in the door hinge area. This loom flexes every time the door opens and is the most common cause of intermittent mirror faults — wiggle the door through its full range while the mirror is selected to see if the fault appears or clears.
- To remove the mirror glass: press firmly at one edge and lever gently — it is clip-mounted onto the adjuster ball. Replacement glass snaps back in. No tools required if you are only replacing the glass.
- Mirror adjustment switch: if no mirrors move in any direction, the switch is suspect. Confirm 12V at the switch input. An OBD scanner with body functions can actuate mirrors directly if the switch is dead — useful for diagnosis.
- ETACS auto-fold setup: the fold-on-lock feature can be enabled or disabled via the customise menu in the instrument cluster (hold OK button on stalk during ignition on sequence — refer to owner handbook). Some personalisation settings require a dealer scan tool.
- A mirror that folds but does not unfold reliably is usually a fold motor with a worn park switch inside — the motor does not know when to stop and stalls.

Engine overheating is a serious condition that can cause head gasket failure, warped cylinder heads, or catastrophic engine damage if ignored. Most overheating is caused by coolant loss (leak or evaporation), failed thermostat, or weak water pump. Diagnosis requires understanding the cooling system components and ruling out causes methodically. Never ignore an overheating warning light or high temperature gauge.

## Symptoms

- Temperature gauge reads above the midpoint or near red zone
- Steam or smoke from under the hood (coolant boiling in radiator)
- Sweet smell from the engine bay (coolant leak or burning)
- Coolant leaking from radiator hose, radiator, or engine block
- Check Engine light illuminated (P0128 - thermostat fault code)
- Loss of heater output (low coolant or airlock in system)
- Engine knocking or pinging under load (pre-ignition due to heat)

## Tools needed

- Coolant thermometer (optional, to measure actual temperatures)
- Compressed air or soft brush to clean radiator fins
- Wrench set for hose clamp removal
- Gasket scraper (if replacing thermostat)
- Funnel and container for coolant

## Tips

- Modern Pajeros have a thermostat that is designed to fail closed (safe) rather than open. If stuck open, the engine will run cold, not hot
- Check the radiator cap — a bad cap cannot hold pressure, causing coolant to boil at a lower temperature. Caps are inexpensive (5-10 dollars) and often overlooked
- If overheating occurs only when towing or in traffic (not on the highway), suspect fan failure or low coolant. Highway driving keeps the engine cooler due to airflow
- Mixing old and new coolant can cause precipitation and blockage. Always flush and refill with fresh coolant when doing major repairs
- If the engine overheats despite all repairs, suspect internal engine damage (cracked head, blown gasket). Have a professional inspect with a pressure test

- **Factory procedure — Cooling System (Section 13)** (M1, section 13)
Upstream O2 sensor — Bank 1 (front right, firewall side)
Bank 1 is the firewall-side bank on the 6G75. $60–140. NGK 92067 is a common aftermarket equivalent.
Upstream O2 sensor — Bank 2 (front left, radiator side)
$60–140. Replace both upstream sensors together if one fails at high mileage.

The 6G75 V6 petrol engine uses four oxygen sensors (two upstream, two downstream — one per bank) to manage fuel trim and monitor catalytic converter efficiency. A faulty O2 sensor causes rich or lean running, increased fuel consumption, and catalyst damage if left unchecked. Fault codes P0130–P0167 (sensor circuit) and P0420/P0430 (catalyst efficiency) are the most common triggers. Sensors typically last 100,000–150,000 km.

## Symptoms

- Check Engine light with P0130–P0167 (O2 sensor circuit fault)
- P0420 or P0430 — catalyst efficiency below threshold (downstream sensor)
- Increased fuel consumption with no other obvious cause
- Rough idle or hunting idle (rich/lean signal causing fuel trim correction)
- Fuel smell from exhaust (overly rich mixture from failed upstream sensor)

## Tools needed

- O2 sensor socket (22mm with wire slot)
- Breaker bar (sensors often seized)
- Anti-seize compound (for installation)
- OBD-II scanner (to clear codes)

## Tips

- Soak sensor threads with penetrating oil (WD-40 or similar) and let it dwell for 30 minutes before removal — sensors seize in the bung after years of heat cycling.
- Replace sensors with the engine cold, not hot — hot metal expands and can strip the thread if the sensor is seized.
- Apply anti-seize to the new sensor threads only — keep it off the last 10 mm near the sensing tip.
- On the 6G75, the upstream sensors are accessible from above (Bank 2, radiator side) and from under the car (Bank 1, firewall side).
- If P0420 appears after replacing only the upstream sensor, the catalyst itself may be damaged. Check downstream sensor voltage before condemning the catalyst.
- After replacement, clear the fault code and drive one full warm-up cycle. The ECU needs to complete its readiness monitors before reporting pass/fail.

Pajero Engine Guide — Which Engine Is In My Vehicle?

The Mitsubishi Pajero (Montero / Shogun) was sold across three generations covered by this site (Gen 2, Gen 3, and Gen 4) with multiple engine options varying by market. Knowing which engine you have is the starting point for finding the right service information, parts numbers, and procedures. This guide summarises all engines by generation and helps you identify yours from the VIN, badge, or engine bay.

## Tips

- When ordering parts, always quote the full model code (e.g. V78W, V97W, V98W) rather than just the year — the same year may have different engines in different markets.
- The Gen 4 4M41 and Gen 3 4M41 share many parts but differ in the ECU mapping, injectors, and some accessory items.
- If you bought a used Pajero without service history, a vehicle history check by VIN or a dealer lookup will confirm the engine fitted.
Loss of Power or Sluggish Acceleration

If your Pajero hesitates, feels sluggish, or won't accelerate above 3000 RPM, the engine is likely in limp-mode or suffering from fuel/air restriction. Power loss can be caused by fuel starvation, air intake blockage, clogged catalytic converter, transmission slipping, or engine timing retard. Diagnosis requires checking fuel pressure, airflow, and scanning for fault codes.

## Symptoms

- Engine feels weak or underpowered, especially under load
- Slow acceleration or inability to reach normal RPM
- Engine cuts power at a specific RPM (often 3000-4000 RPM, limp-mode threshold)
- Check Engine light illuminated
- Black smoke from exhaust (overfueling), white smoke (lean or coolant), or blue smoke (oil burn)
- Transmission seems to slip or not hold gears (but may be normal if engine is weak)
- Hesitation when merging on highway or climbing hills

## Tools needed

- OBD2 scan tool to read fault codes
- Fuel pressure gauge (to check fuel system)
- Vacuum gauge (to check exhaust backpressure)
- Air filter (for inspection/replacement)
- Flashlight and mirror (to inspect filter and hoses)

## Tips

- Fuel octane rating matters on the 6G75 petrol. Using 87-octane fuel in a vehicle tuned for 91-octane causes knock and power loss. Switch to premium fuel and retest
- Turbo lag is normal on the 4M41 diesel; low-RPM power is weak until boost builds. If this feels unusually sluggish, suspect boost leak
- Transmission fluid condition directly affects feel of acceleration. Even if engine is strong, a worn transmission feels powerless
- If power loss appears suddenly after a gas station fill-up, suspect bad fuel or water in tank. Drain tank and refill with fuel from a different station

Power Steering Problems — Fluid, Leaks & Whine
Dexron II/III ATF or Mitsubishi Genuine PSF

The Pajero IV uses hydraulic power steering with a belt-driven pump and a steering rack. Common problems are fluid leaks (low fluid causing whine), a worn pump, and rack seal failure. Power steering fluid is rarely changed but should be inspected at every service — dark or burnt fluid indicates oxidation and accelerates pump wear. Heavy or stiff steering is a safety concern and should be addressed promptly.

## Symptoms

- Whining or groaning noise when turning the steering wheel
- Steering heavier than normal, especially at low speed
- Power steering fluid level dropping regularly (leak)
- Fluid puddle under the engine bay (usually front-left area)
- Steering wheel vibrating or shuddering when turned
- Fluid foam or bubbles visible in reservoir (air in system)

## Tips

- Check the power steering fluid reservoir first — it is a small cap-and-dipstick unit near the firewall. Level should be between MIN and MAX with the engine warm.
- Whining that increases with steering input usually means low fluid. Top up and check for leaks before assuming pump failure.
- Inspect the power steering hoses (high-pressure from pump, low-pressure return) for weeping at connections — these are the most common leak points.
- The power steering rack seal is a common failure point on 100,000+ km vehicles. Oil seeping from the rubber boots on the steering rack confirms rack seal failure.
- Do not overfill the reservoir — foaming from overfill causes air entrainment which accelerates pump wear.
- A pump that whines only on cold startup and quiets when warm usually just needs fresh fluid. A pump that whines constantly with correct fluid level is worn.
- Power steering fluid is Dexron II/III ATF or Mitsubishi Genuine PSF — do not use DOT brake fluid or any other type.

The Pajero IV is a capable and reliable vehicle when maintained, but specific failure points become expensive at high mileage. This checklist covers the known weak spots to inspect before buying a used example. A professional pre-purchase inspection ($150–200 at an independent mechanic) is always worthwhile, but walking through this list yourself first filters out the worst examples before paying for a formal check.

## Symptoms

- Any of the following checks revealing a fault — use as a go/no-go filter before committing

## Tips

- ENGINE — Check oil filler cap for creamy or mayonnaise-like residue (coolant in oil = head gasket or oil cooler fault). Any milky contamination is a serious warning.
- ENGINE — Check coolant colour in the overflow reservoir. Mitsubishi Pajero IV uses blue HOAT coolant. If it is orange, green, or brown, the coolant has been mixed incorrectly — accelerated corrosion of the cooling system is likely.
- ENGINE — With the engine warm and idling, watch for white smoke from the exhaust that persists after warm-up. Persistent white smoke = coolant burning. Black smoke at idle = EGR or injector issue.
- ENGINE — Ask about EGR cleaning history on 4M41 diesel models. Carbon buildup in the EGR and intake manifold is universal on these engines — if it has never been cleaned at 100,000+ km, budget for the job.
- ENGINE — Check the crankshaft pulley (harmonic balancer) for signs of the rubber ring delaminating or the pulley wobbling. Crankshaft pulley failure is a known Pajero IV fault — a failed pulley throws belts and can cause accessory drive and alternator failure.
- TRANSMISSION — Test all 4WD modes: 2H, 4H, 4HLc, 4LLc. All should engage cleanly within 2–3 seconds with no grinding. Flashing 4WD light indicates a mode that has not fully engaged.
- TRANSMISSION — On automatics, check for smooth, firm gear changes at low speed, cruise, and hard acceleration. Delayed engagement from Park to Drive, or slipping between gears, indicates worn clutch packs.
- DRIVETRAIN — Drive slowly in a tight circle in each direction and listen for clicking from the front wheels — CV joint wear. Any clicking on full lock needs investigation.
- DRIVETRAIN — On models with rear diff lock: test it on a loose surface (gravel or grass). One rear wheel should spin while the other stays stationary (unlocked); with the lock engaged, both should turn at the same speed.
- SUSPENSION — Check all four shock absorbers for oil leaks (wet or oily around the shaft seal). Bounce each corner — the vehicle should settle after one oscillation, not continue bouncing.
- SUSPENSION — Listen for clunks or knocking over bumps at slow speed (5–20 km/h). Clunking indicates worn suspension bushings, ball joints, or sway bar links.
- BRAKES — Brake from 60 km/h — the vehicle should stop straight with no pulling, pulsing, or grinding. ABS sensors fail on Pajero IVs used in dusty conditions; an ABS light on the dash with no other faults is a common inexpensive fix.
- BODY — Inspect door sills, rear wheel arches, and the area under the rear bumper for rust. Pajero IVs used near the coast or on unsealed roads with no undersealing are prone to corrosion in these areas.
- BODY — Check all four windows go up and down fully. Slow or jerky operation = worn regulator. Window that stops mid-travel = motor or fuse.
- ELECTRONICS — Check all dashboard warning lights extinguish after start. A 4WD, ABS, or airbag light remaining on indicates a known fault.
- ELECTRONICS — Test the air conditioning. On 6G75 petrol models the AC compressor is driven by the serpentine belt; on 4M41 diesel, check cold air output and listen for compressor clutch noise.
- SERVICE HISTORY — Ask for evidence of timing belt replacement on 4M41 diesel models. The factory interval is 90,000 km — if it is not in the history, it should be budgeted immediately. A snapped timing belt is a catastrophic engine failure.
- TAKATA AIRBAG RECALL — Pajero IV (NS/NT/NW/NX models) were subject to a mandatory Takata airbag inflator recall. Verify the recall has been completed via the NEVDIS/VIN check system in Australia, or the relevant recall database in your country. Do not purchase a vehicle with an outstanding airbag recall.

The Pajero IV has two main radiator hoses — upper and lower — plus a smaller overflow/header tank hose. Rubber hoses degrade and eventually crack or split, causing catastrophic coolant loss. They should be inspected at every service (squeeze the hose when cold — it should feel firm and spring back, not mushy or hard and cracked). A 10-year or 150,000 km replacement interval is conservative but sensible for a vehicle used off-road.

## Symptoms

- Coolant leak visible at hose or hose clamp connection
- Hose feels soft and mushy when squeezed cold (internal delamination)
- Hose is hard and cracked when squeezed (heat-hardened, failure imminent)
- Engine temperature rising faster than normal (restricted hose reducing flow)
- Coolant smell from engine bay without visible leak (seeping past clamp)

## Tools needed

- Pliers or hose clamp tool (spring clamps)
- Drain pan (minimum 3L)
- Hose pick or blunt knife (to break hose seal)

## Tips

- Always replace hoses with the engine cold — never open the cooling system when hot.
- To break the seal of a stuck hose: twist it before pulling, or carefully use a hose pick around the rim. Do not pry with a screwdriver — the plastic thermostat housing and radiator neck are fragile.
- Check the hose clamps when replacing hoses — original spring-type clamps weaken over time. Replace with screw-type clamps for better long-term retention.
- Burp the coolant system after refilling: leave the heater on full, start the engine, and wait until the top hose gets hot (thermostat opens). Then check the level again and top up if needed.
- Inspect the header/overflow tank hoses at the same time — they are small in diameter and often overlooked.
- If replacing only one hose, check the age and condition of the other before reassembly. Labour is the same either way.

The Pajero IV rear axle has sealed hub bearings that support the wheel and transmit drive from the differential to each wheel. The bearings are sealed for life — they cannot be repacked, but can be inspected for play and wear. Bearing failure is rare under normal driving but increases with off-road abuse. Symptoms are a grinding noise when cornering or a rhythmic clicking/clunking at highway speed. Hub bearing inspection is simple and should be part of every major service, especially after off-road trips or high-mileage accumulation (150,000+ km).

## Symptoms

- Grinding or growling noise from rear axle that changes pitch with speed
- Clicking or popping sound during hard cornering (wheel bearing loading)
- Vibration through the chassis that increases with speed (wheel imbalance or bearing play)
- Loose or notchy feeling in the wheel when spun by hand (bearing preload worn)
- Excessive play in the wheel when lifted (check lateral movement at the 3 and 9 o'clock positions)
- Dust or grease around the wheel seal area (bearing seal failure — internal contamination)

## Tools needed

- Jack and jack stands (to safely lift the vehicle)
- Wrench or socket for wheel bolts (usually 17mm)
- Torque wrench

## Tips

- Wheel bearing inspection is a visual and tactile check — you are feeling for play and listening for noise, not measuring anything with a dial gauge.
- Jack the rear axle (not a wheel), support with jack stands under the frame, and spin each wheel by hand. Listen for grinding. Grasp the wheel at 3 and 9 o'clock and try to wiggle it — there should be no play (wheel should not move side-to-side).
- A small amount of up-and-down play is normal (less than 2mm), but any side-to-side movement or grinding noise indicates bearing wear.
- The sealed bearing cartridge is a complete unit — there is no adjustment or maintenance. If play is detected, the hub assembly must be replaced by pressing out the old bearing and pressing in a new one. This is a workshop job.
- After off-road driving, check bearings more frequently (every 20,000 km) — mud, water, and rough terrain can accelerate bearing wear.
- Do not ignore early warning signs: a slight grinding that comes and goes may suddenly worsen during a long highway drive, leading to a catastrophic failure with the wheel locking up.
- Never attempt to open or repack a sealed hub bearing. If you suspect bearing damage, the hub must be replaced as a complete assembly. Partial disassembly will destroy the bearing immediately.

## Factory procedure reference

- **Factory procedure — Rear Axle (Section 27)** (M1, section 27)
Rear Axle Noise — Whining, Clunking & Grinding
If noise started after sitting idle or in cold weather, low oil level may be the cause. Refill to the filler plug level and retest. $20–30.
If gear noise persists after oil change, differential overhaul or replacement is required
This is a major repair ($1,500–2,500+ labour). First confirm diagnosis with a scan tool and oil analysis.

Rear differential noise often begins as a subtle whine and progresses through clunking to grinding if ignored. The noises come from worn gears, low oil level, bearing wear, or play between the differential case and the housing. Early diagnosis is critical — a small whine may be corrected by an oil change, but a loud grinding often means metal-to-metal contact and imminent failure. Noise character and when it occurs (acceleration, coasting, cornering) help identify the source.

## Symptoms

- High-pitched whining from the rear axle during acceleration (worn gears or low oil)
- Whining that increases with speed and changes pitch (bearing wear or differential starvation)
- Clunking or banging from the axle during hard acceleration (driveshaft play or loose axle bracket)
- Grinding noise when turning sharply (bearing load test or worn differential)
- Noise that gets louder over weeks or months (progressive gear wear)
- Knocking from the axle on bumps or hard braking (worn engine/transmission mount affecting driveshaft angle, not necessarily axle fault)

## Tools needed

- Multimeter (to test for differential lock system voltage interference)
- OBD-II scanner (to read any active codes related to differential pressure or lock status)
- Oil sample kit (to send drained oil for metal particle analysis)

## Tips

- The first step is always to check the differential oil level and condition. A noisy differential with dark, burnt-smelling oil is an oil starvation problem — low level or degraded oil viscosity. Change the oil and re-test before assuming mechanical failure.
- Differentiate between bearing noise and gear noise: bearing noise is a grinding or growl that changes with cornering load (louder on tight turns). Gear noise is a whine that changes with engine load (louder on hard acceleration).
- Whining during acceleration specifically points to gear mesh wear (pinion and ring gear contact deteriorating). Whining at cruise on a level road points to bearing wear (load-dependent). Whining at any engine speed (even idle) suggests differential starvation from low oil.
- Clunking or banging during hard acceleration often comes from U-joint or driveshaft play, not the differential itself. Isolate the noise by coasting (no engine torque) — if the noise disappears, it's likely the driveshaft or transmission mount, not the rear axle.
- If the noise is reproducible with the vehicle parked (engine running, no load on the differential), you may have a pump whine from the differential lock system, not bearing/gear wear. Listen carefully for a separate electric motor whine.
- Test at different loads: gentle acceleration on flat ground, hard acceleration, cornering under load, and coasting. Each load condition causes different noise, helping pinpoint the source.
- Once a gear or bearing has begun to fail, the damage accelerates exponentially. A vehicle that whines quietly today may be unable to drive tomorrow if the noise comes from advanced bearing wear.
- Differential lock engagement sometimes masks underlying noise because locking stiffens the whole axle assembly. If the differential lock makes the noise disappear temporarily, the underlying issue (bearing play or gear wear) is still present and will worsen when the lock is disengaged.

## Factory procedure reference

- **Factory procedure — Rear Axle (Section 27)** (M1, section 27)
Rear Differential Lock — Operation, Diagnosis, and Maintenance

The Pajero's locking rear differential (rear diff lock or RDL) mechanically locks the left and right rear axles together for maximum traction in extreme off-road conditions. When locked, both rear wheels turn at the same speed regardless of grip, eliminating the open-differential tendency to spin the wheel with the least traction. The system changed across generations — Gen 2/3 use a vacuum-actuated system, while Gen 4 integrates Active Stability Control (ATSC) management. Knowing how it works and common failure points helps diagnose a diff lock that won't engage or disengage.

## Symptoms

- Diff lock warning lamp flashes but never goes solid (engagement failure)
- Diff lock lamp stays on after disengaging (stuck engaged)
- No response when diff lock button is pressed
- Grinding or clunking when attempting to engage or disengage at speed
- Vehicle pulling to one side on a straight paved road (diff lock engaged by mistake)

## Tips

- On pavement with the diff lock accidentally engaged, you will feel tight steering and may hear tyre scrub in turns — disengage immediately.
- Always disengage the rear diff lock before driving on paved roads or at speed. Driving with it locked on pavement causes severe drivetrain stress.
- If the diff lock won't disengage on a slope, try rocking the vehicle forwards and backwards while operating the switch.
- Gen 2/3 vacuum lines are often the culprit after 20+ years — budget for a full vacuum hose replacement if the system is acting erratically.
Rear Differential Lock — Air Pump & Diagnosis
Rear differential lock air pump (12V DC)
Electric air pump with integrated pressure switch
Replace if pump runs but produces no air pressure. $150–350 depending on core exchange.
2-position solenoid-operated air valve, mounted on axle housing
Replace if pump runs but lock does not engage. $50–120.
Mechanical or electronic switch indicating lock position
Replace if lock engages but indicator light doesn't illuminate. $30–80.
Air line kit (pump to rear axle)
Flexible nylon air tubing with compression fittings
Inspect for cracks, kinks, or detached fittings. Replacement line kits ~$40–60.

The Pajero IV's rear differential has an optional air-operated locking differential that allows both rear wheels to spin together for maximum traction in deep mud or rock crawling. The system uses a 12V DC pump, air lines, and a solenoid-operated air lock chamber. Without the lock engaged, the differential spins wheels independently (normal on-road mode). The lock can fail silently — the light on the dash shows status, but diagnosis requires checking air pressure, pump operation, and electrical signals. A seized or failed air pump is the most common fault.

## Symptoms

- Differential lock light on the dashboard flashing or not responding after selector
- Lock engages but will not disengage (stuck open)
- Hissing or whistling from the rear axle area when lock attempts engagement
- Rear diff lock unavailable during off-road operation even though selector was moved
- Slow response to lock engagement request (pump sluggish or weak)
- Grinding noise when lock engages or disengages (mechanical seizure)

## Tools needed

- Multimeter (to check voltage at pump and solenoid)
- OBD-II scanner (to read differential lock control codes)
- Air pressure gauge (to test pump output pressure)
- 13mm socket (solenoid and detection switch removal)

## Tips

- Check the dashboard indicator first: solid light means lock is engaged, flashing means lock signal is being sent but mechanical engagement is incomplete, no light means electrical signal not reaching the solenoid.
- The rear differential lock is a luxury feature, not essential for normal driving — if it fails, the vehicle remains safely drivable in 2H or 4H without locking.
- The air pump draws power directly from the battery through a relay in the engine bay fuse box. A blown fuse cuts power to the pump immediately — check this before assuming pump failure.
- To test the pump: activate the selector (you will hear a brief 'click' in the engine bay as the relay closes). If no sound and the indicator light doesn't come on, the relay or fuse has failed.
- If the pump runs (audible whining in the engine bay) but the light doesn't illuminate, the solenoid on the rear axle is not receiving signal — check the differential lock detection switch (located on the rear axle assembly).
- Air line failure: if a line has cracked or detached, air pressure will be lost and the lock will not hold. Inspect lines running along the frame from the pump to the rear axle. Listen for hissing with the lock engaged.
- Do not attempt to drive aggressively on-road with the lock engaged — locking the differential puts extreme stress on driveshafts and suspension. Lock should only be engaged when off-road or in extreme low-speed conditions (below 5 km/h).
- After off-road use, always unlock the differential immediately on solid ground before returning to paved roads — continuous locking on asphalt causes tyre scrub and driveline damage.

The Pajero IV rear suspension uses a multi-link arrangement with trailing arms, toe control arms, upper arms, and a stabiliser bar — all connected via rubber bushings and ball joints. Worn bushings cause vague handling, rear-end wandering, and clunking over bumps. Ball joint wear causes play and can be detected by lifting the wheel and checking for movement. Bushing replacement requires pressing out the old rubber and pressing in new ones, which typically needs a hydraulic press — this is a workshop job, but inspection is straightforward.

## Symptoms

- Clunking or knocking from the rear over bumps or during hard braking
- Rear-end wander or loose feeling on straight roads
- Uneven rear tyre wear (worn bushings change toe angle)
- Squeaking from the rear suspension during slow manoeuvring
- Visible cracking or splitting in rubber bushings (inspect during service)
- Play detected when lifting the rear wheel and pushing/pulling the tyre

## Tools needed

- Jack and axle stands
- 17mm socket (wheel bolts)
- Pry bar (to check ball joint play with wheel loaded)
- Ball joint separator (pickle fork) for removal
- Hydraulic press (for bushing replacement — workshop tool)
- Torque wrench

## Tips

- All suspension arm bolts must be torqued with the vehicle at ride height (on the ground or on a ramp with the suspension loaded). Torquing at full droop pre-loads the bushings and causes premature failure.
- Bushing inspection: look for visible rubber cracking, collapse (the bush has sagged and there is a gap around it), or separation (the inner metal sleeve is moving independently). Any of these means replacement.
- Ball joint play check: lift the rear axle (not individual wheels) so both rear wheels hang freely. Grip each tyre at 12 and 6 o'clock and push/pull — check for movement in the toe arm joint. Grip at 3 and 9 o'clock to check the upper arm joint.
- Stabiliser link turning torque: can be checked with a torque wrench — the ball joint should rotate with a specific resistance (typically 0.5–3.0 Nm). Zero resistance means the ball has worn loose.
- Rear wheel alignment: whenever any rear suspension component is replaced, a four-wheel alignment is required. Rear toe and camber are adjustable on the Pajero IV and shift when arms are disturbed.
- Polyurethane bushing upgrades are popular for off-road use — they resist oil contamination and last longer than rubber. However, they transmit more noise and vibration into the cabin. Use standard rubber for a daily driver.

A rough or shaky idle at a stoplight or in neutral indicates an imbalance in combustion. The 4M41 diesel should idle smoothly at 650-750 RPM; the 6G75 petrol at 600-800 RPM. If the engine vibrates, stutters, or threatens to stall, a systematic diagnosis can pinpoint whether the issue is ignition, fuel delivery, air intake, or mechanical wear. Most rough idle fixes are inexpensive (spark plugs, fuel injector cleaning) and can prevent more serious damage.

## Symptoms

- Engine shakes or vibrates noticeably at idle (felt through steering wheel or seat)
- RPM fluctuates (bounces between 500-900 RPM instead of steady)
- Engine sounds like it might stall but catches itself
- Rough idle disappears when accelerating (suggests fuel trim or cylinder imbalance)
- Check Engine light illuminated (P0300 or P0301-P0306 misfire codes)
- White or black smoke from exhaust when idling (bad combustion)
- Hesitation when shifting into Drive (load aggravates the condition)

## Tools needed

- OBD2 scan tool to read fault codes
- Fuel pressure gauge (optional, for fuel system checks)
- Spark plug gap tool (6G75 petrol)
- Soapy water spray (to find vacuum leaks)
- Compression gauge (optional, to diagnose mechanical wear)

## Tips

- Rough idle is rarely dangerous, but ignoring it can lead to worse misfires and poor fuel economy
- If rough idle is intermittent (comes and goes), suspect a failing sensor (MAF, O2) rather than worn plugs
- [object Object]
- If rough idle persists after spark plug replacement (6G75), suspect coil pack or fuel injector, not the plugs
- Some rough idle can be caused by a failing transmission torque converter (6G75 auto). The converter allows the engine to load differently at idle
- Diesel engines (4M41) often have slight vibration at idle due to the combustion process. Some roughness is normal; compare to a known-good vehicle

Shock absorbers dampen suspension movement, keeping the vehicle stable during braking, cornering, and rough roads. The 4M41 and 6G75 Pajero models use telescopic shock absorbers both front and rear. Worn shocks (60,000–100,000 km lifespan) cause bouncy ride quality, reduced cornering stability, extended braking distance, and accelerated wear on tires and suspension bushings. Replacement is straightforward and can be done at home with common tools, though alignment check is recommended after front shock replacement.

## Symptoms

- Vehicle bounces excessively after driving over bumps (oscillates 3+ times instead of settling in 1)
- Noticeable body roll during cornering or lane changes (loss of dampening)
- Uneven tire wear, especially inner/outer edges (unstable suspension geometry)
- Reduced braking stability or extended stopping distance (suspension not controlling weight transfer)
- Fluid leak visible on shock body (seal failure, shock cannot be repaired)
- Clunking or squeaking noise from suspension during turns or over bumps

## Tools needed

- Socket set and wrenches (usually 12-16mm for shock mounting bolts)
- Jack and jack stands (minimum 2-ton capacity jack to safely lift vehicle and remove wheels)
- Wheel chocks (wooden blocks or purchased chocks to prevent vehicle from rolling while suspended)
- Shock removal tool or penetrating oil like WD-40 or Liquid Wrench for stubborn mounting bolts
- Torque wrench with 50-100 Nm range for mounting bolts

## Tips

- Do NOT attempt to compress the shock by hand or with a vice. Shocks contain pressurized nitrogen gas (on some models) and can explode if incorrectly handled
- When the old shock is removed, it may still feel slightly bouncy due to residual gas pressure. This is normal. Dispose of it as hazardous waste at a recycling center
- If the mounting bolts are severely corroded (common in salt-belt climates), spray with penetrating oil 30 minutes before removal and use a socket with a cheater bar for additional leverage
- Many people replace front shocks and forget the rear, or vice versa. For best handling, replace all four shocks in one go. Mismatched dampening causes uneven ride and handling
- Aftermarket shocks (Bilstein, KYB, Monroe) are available in sport (firmer) or comfort (softer) versions. Choose based on your driving preference
- After installation, get a wheel alignment done (front end only, for front shock replacement). Misalignment causes uneven tire wear and poor handling
- Some aftermarket shocks come with different mounting hardware. Ensure bolts match the original spec before installation
- The upper shock mounting point is inside the wheel well, but the bolt extends through the shock tower on top of the engine bay. You may need to remove the plastic engine cover or wheel well liner for access
