The 6G75 petrol engine has six spark plugs, one per cylinder. Factory service intervals recommend replacement every 40,000 km for standard copper plugs, or every 100,000 km for premium platinum/iridium plugs (which cost more upfront but last longer). Worn plugs increase fuel consumption, reduce starting reliability, and cause hesitation under acceleration. The job is straightforward — no special tools required beyond a spark plug socket.

## Symptoms

- Scheduled maintenance interval (40,000 km for copper, 100,000 km for premium)
- Difficulty starting, especially in cold weather
- Rough idle or stumbling during acceleration
- Visible spark plug fouling (black soot, oil residue, or gap erosion when inspected)
- Reduced fuel economy or loss of performance
- Slight hesitation when upshifting under light load

## Tools needed

- Spark plug socket (usually 16mm, with rubber insert to protect the ceramic insulator)
- Socket wrench and ratchet
- Spark plug gap tool or feeler gauge (to check gap before installation)
- Wire stripper or coil pack removal tool (if needed to access plugs under coils)
- Clean soft-bristle brush (to clean around spark plug holes)
- Engine cover removal tools (plastic clips, if present)

## Tips

- The spark plugs are located under six individual coil packs on top of the engine (one coil per cylinder). Each coil sits directly on a spark plug.
- Remove one coil at a time by unclipping the connector and twisting the coil counterclockwise. The spark plug sits directly underneath.
- Inspect the spark plug wire or coil boot for cracks, corrosion, or oil residue. Clean if dirty; replace if damaged.
- If a spark plug breaks inside the hole during installation, do not force it. Back out carefully with pliers or a screw extractor.
- Check the gap on new plugs before installation. Most come pre-gapped to 1.0mm, but factory settings can vary.
- [object Object]
- After replacing all six, start the engine and listen for any unusual knocking. The engine may run slightly rough for 1–2 minutes as the ECU adapts to the new plugs.
- If the Check Engine light appears after replacement, scan for misfires (P0300–P0306) and verify coil-to-plug connections are seated firmly.

Starting and charging faults are among the most common reasons a Pajero IV won't start or dies unexpectedly. The system has three key components: the battery (stores energy), the alternator (recharges while running), and the starter motor (cranks the engine). A fault in any one of them produces similar symptoms — this guide walks through isolating which component is the cause.

## Symptoms

- Engine won't crank at all (dead silence when turning key)
- Single click when turning key (starter solenoid engaging but motor not spinning)
- Rapid clicking when turning key (battery too low to hold starter voltage)
- Engine cranks slowly — weak, laboured rotation
- Engine starts fine when warm but struggles after sitting overnight
- Battery warning light on dashboard while driving
- Headlights dim at idle, brighten when revved

## Tips

- Start with a voltage test: battery at rest should be 12.5–12.7V fully charged. Below 12.0V means the battery is discharged or failing.
- With engine running, measure battery terminals. 13.2–14.4V = alternator charging. Below 13.0V = alternator fault or high electrical load.
- Jump-starting solves a flat battery but not a failing alternator — if the vehicle dies again after being jump-started, the alternator is the fault.
- Clicking or no-crank with a known-good battery points to the starter motor or its solenoid.
- Corroded battery terminals cause 20–30% of apparent battery failures. Clean them before buying a new battery.
- Cold weather reduces battery capacity by 30–50%. A battery that works fine in summer may fail its first cold morning.
- A battery older than 4 years in a hot climate should be load-tested, not just voltage-tested — voltage can look normal but capacity is gone.
Replace if play is detected or rubber boot is torn. $30–80 each. Always replace in pairs.
Inspect at every service. Replace if torn — prevents water and grit reaching the rack. $15–30.
Replace if steering feels imprecise or has oscillation after kerb strike. $50–120.

Steering system maintenance on the Pajero IV covers the power steering fluid, steering rack boots, tie rod ends, and steering damper. These components are inspected at major services but rarely replaced on schedule — most are replaced when they show wear. A well-maintained steering system gives precise, responsive handling; neglected components cause wandering, play, and vibration.

## Symptoms

- Excessive play or looseness in the steering wheel (worn tie rods or rack)
- Vehicle wanders on straight roads (alignment or worn steering components)
- Clunking over bumps from steering area (worn steering damper or ball joints)
- Torn steering rack boots (inspect visually at every service)

## Tools needed

- Torque wrench
- 17mm, 19mm spanners (tie rod lock nut)
- Ball joint separator / pickle fork

## Tips

- Always get a wheel alignment after replacing tie rod ends — even a small change in tie rod length shifts the toe setting.
- Check tie rod end play by gripping the tyre at 9 and 3 o'clock and pushing/pulling. Any detectable knock is a worn end — replace it.
- Steering rack boots protect the rack from grit and moisture. Torn boots lead to rack seal failure within 12–24 months. They cost $15 to replace.
- The steering damper is often forgotten — it is located across the front axle and dampens kickback from rough terrain. A failed damper causes steering wheel wobble on corrugated roads.

## Factory procedure reference

- **Factory procedure — Power Steering (Section 37)** (M1, section 37)

Suspension noises — squeaks, creaks, or clunks — typically indicate worn bushings, loose bolts, or failed dampers (shocks). Unlike engine knocks, suspension noises are often intermittent and depend on driving conditions (rough road, turning, braking). Most suspension noises are not safety-critical but warrant investigation to prevent secondary damage (e.g., a loose sway bar link can wear a hole in the steering knuckle if left unaddressed). This guide walks you through identifying the source and determining repair urgency.

## Tips

- Many DIY mechanics mistake suspension noises for engine knocks. If the noise is clearly from the wheel wells and not under the hood, it is suspension

The thermostat controls coolant flow to maintain the engine at operating temperature (~88°C). A stuck-open thermostat causes P0128 (engine runs cold), poor fuel economy, and a heater that never gets hot. A stuck-closed thermostat causes overheating. Replacement requires a partial coolant drain and takes about an hour. Always use a new gasket and refill with the correct coolant.

## Symptoms

- Temperature gauge stuck low or slow to reach midpoint (stuck open)
- Overheating — gauge climbing above midpoint (stuck closed)
- P0128 fault code stored
- Heater not producing warm air even when engine is warm
- Poor fuel economy (cold engine runs richer)

## Tools needed

- 10mm socket and ratchet (thermostat housing bolts)
- Flat-head screwdriver (hose clamps)
- Drain pan (minimum 3L)
- Torque wrench

## Tips

- Drain only 2–3 litres of coolant from the lower radiator hose — you do not need to fully drain the system.
- Clean the thermostat housing sealing surface before fitting the new gasket. Aluminium surfaces corrode and leave bumps that cause leaks.
- After refilling, run the engine with the heater on full and the cap off the expansion tank. Squeeze the upper radiator hose to help purge air bubbles.
- A stuck-open thermostat is much more common than a stuck-closed one — the spring mechanism wears open, not shut.
- If the temperature gauge reads normal but P0128 is stored, the coolant temperature sensor may be the actual fault — check sensor resistance before replacing the thermostat.

The 6G75 petrol engine is an interference engine — if the timing belt breaks, pistons will collide with open valves, causing catastrophic internal damage (bent valves, damaged piston crowns, broken rings). Factory service intervals call for replacement every 100,000 km or 10 years, whichever comes first. This is a complex, time-consuming job requiring cylinder head removal, specialized tools, and precise timing procedures. Most owners have this performed at a dealership or experienced independent shop. The 4M41 diesel does NOT have a timing belt (uses a chain), so diesel owners can ignore this procedure.

## Symptoms

- Scheduled maintenance interval (100,000 km or 10 years, whichever comes first)
- Visual inspection shows cracking, fraying, or separation of the belt rubber
- Visible oil leaks from the timing cover (water pump or cover gasket deterioration)
- [object Object]
- Engine misfire or rough idle (timing has drifted due to belt wear)
- Previous owner records indicate original belt has never been replaced

## Tools needed

- Timing belt tool kit with crankshaft and camshaft synchronization tools (specialized, most shops own these)
- Cylinder head torque wrench with 20-100 Nm range, must be accurate to +/- 5 percent
- Socket set and wrenches with specialty bits for timing cover bolts
- Jack and jack stands with minimum 2-ton capacity to lift vehicle and access front end

## Tips

- This job involves cylinder head removal and is beyond the scope of a typical weekend DIY project. Mistakes result in bent valves or misaligned timing, both catastrophic failures.
- The factory procedure spans several pages and requires multiple specialized tools. Strongly recommend professional shop installation.
- [object Object]
- After installation, the engine may not start on the first try. This is normal — air in fuel lines (if the pump was disturbed) may prevent ignition. Prime the fuel system by cycling the ignition key without cranking, or crank for 10 seconds and rest, repeat 2–3 times.
- Always replace the tensioner, water pump, and all timing cover gaskets in one job. The labor cost is negligible compared to pulling the engine again if these items fail prematurely.
- Do not attempt this job without access to a service manual and proper timing tools. Many independent shops have these tools and charge $1,200–$1,800 in labor. A botched DIY job can cost $3,000+ in engine damage repairs.

Correct tire pressure and regular wear inspection are the easiest preventive maintenance tasks and have the biggest impact on safety, fuel economy, and tire life. Under-inflated tires generate excessive heat and wear rapidly; over-inflated tires lose grip and can fail suddenly. Wear patterns reveal suspension and alignment issues. Tire pressure should be checked monthly and before long trips. The door jamb label on the driver's side shows the factory-recommended pressures for your vehicle.

## Symptoms

- Tire pressure warning light on dashboard (some vehicles have TPMS)
- Uneven or excessive tire wear on one edge or side
- Cupping (scalloped wear pattern) suggesting suspension or imbalance issue
- Bulge or flat spot on the tire sidewall (impact damage or internal failure)
- Tire tread depth below 1.6 mm (legal minimum in most countries; 3 mm is safer for wet conditions)
- Pulling to one side during straight-line driving (alignment issue)

## Tools needed

- Tire pressure gauge
- Tread depth gauge or penny (for wear measurement)
- Jack and jack stands (if rotating tires)

## Tips

- Check tire pressure when cold: at least 3 hours after the last drive, or before driving. Pressure rises 5–10 kPa per 10°C increase in temperature while driving, giving a false high reading if you check immediately after driving.
- Tread depth test with a penny: insert a penny upside-down into the tread. If you can see the top of the penny, tread is below 1.6 mm and the tire is legally worn out. For safety in wet conditions, replace at 3 mm (where you can barely see the top of the penny).
- Wear pattern diagnosis: Wear on both outer edges (centre tread intact) indicates over-inflation. Wear in the centre (edges intact) indicates under-inflation. Wear on one edge only indicates alignment issue or one side of suspension sagging. Scalloped or cupped wear (rhythmic dips) suggests wheel imbalance or suspension wear (worn shocks, ball joints).
- Tire rotation (front ↔ rear every 20,000 km) extends tire life by 10–20%. The front tires wear faster on most vehicles, so rotation distributes wear evenly.
- Seasonal pressure change: tire pressure drops ~1 kPa per 1°C drop in ambient temperature. In winter, expect pressure to drop 20–30 kPa; in summer, it may rise 20–30 kPa. Adjust accordingly.
- Off-road pressure reduction (for sand, mud, or very rough terrain) increases the tire contact patch for traction. Never reduce below 200 kPa, as rim damage may occur. Always re-inflate to normal pressure before driving on pavement.
- Typical Pajero IV tire size: 265/70R16 or 285/75R16. Load index and speed rating are printed on the sidewall (e.g., 112H = 1060 kg per tire, H = 210 km/h max). Always replace with the same size unless upgrading to a larger approved size (check owner's manual). Mixing tire brands, tread patterns, or sizes can cause handling issues and uneven wear.

The Pajero IV is rated for 3,000 kg braked towing — one of the highest figures in its class. However, the towing limits involve several interacting figures (GVM, GCM, towball mass) that are easy to exceed without realising. Exceeding any one of them voids insurance and may be illegal. This guide covers the key numbers, what they mean, and what to check before towing.

## Tips

- Braked towing capacity: 3,000 kg — the maximum trailer weight when the trailer has its own brakes. This applies when towing at 3,000 kg with a maximum towball mass (TBM) of 180 kg.
- Alternative rating: 2,500 kg braked with a towball mass of up to 250 kg. If your trailer tongue weight exceeds 180 kg, you must stay within the 2,500 kg trailer limit.
- Unbraked towing capacity: 750 kg — the maximum trailer weight when the trailer has no brakes. Most trailers over 750 kg are required by law to have brakes fitted.
- Towball mass (TBM): the downward force the trailer exerts on the tow ball. Too little TBM causes trailer sway; too much overloads the rear axle. Aim for 8–10% of trailer GVM. On a 2,500 kg trailer that is 200–250 kg — check your trailer.
- GVM (Gross Vehicle Mass): 3,030 kg — the maximum total weight of the Pajero including passengers, fuel, luggage, and towball download. Towball download counts toward GVM. With a full load of passengers and gear, it is easy to exceed GVM before you even hitch the trailer.
- GCM (Gross Combination Mass): 6,030 kg — the maximum combined weight of the loaded Pajero and loaded trailer. GCM = your Pajero's actual weight + trailer's actual weight. Do not confuse GCM with towing capacity.
- Trailer brakes required: trailers over 750 kg unloaded (or over 2,000 kg loaded, jurisdiction-dependent) must have brakes. Over 2,000 kg, all trailer wheels must have brakes and an automatic breakaway system is required.
- Tow hitch rating: the towbar fitted to the vehicle must be rated to at least match the trailer weight. Check the towbar's compliance plate — some aftermarket towbars are rated lower than the vehicle's factory limit.
- Mirrors: if the trailer is wider than the vehicle, extended towing mirrors are legally required in most jurisdictions. Standard Pajero IV mirrors are adequate for most trailers up to 2.3 m wide.
- Electrical: the Pajero IV uses a 7-pin flat trailer plug (Australian standard) or 13-pin Euro connector depending on market. Confirm your trailer plug matches before hitching.

The transfer case distributes drive to front and rear axles and enables low-range 4WD. It uses its own fluid, separate from the transmission. The Pajero IV transfer case takes approximately 1.1L of ATF SP-III (same fluid as the automatic transmission). This is the most commonly forgotten fluid service on 4WD vehicles — neglect causes premature wear of the chain or gear set inside. Service interval: every 40,000 km or whenever the fluid is dark or contaminated.

## Symptoms

- Routine service interval reached (40,000 km)
- Vibration or whine from transfer case area in 4WD
- 4WD engagement grinding or hesitation
- Dark or metallic-looking fluid found on inspection

## Tools needed

- 17mm hex key / Allen socket (drain and fill plugs)
- Drain pan (minimum 2L)
- Torque wrench
- Hand-operated fluid pump (for refilling through the side filler hole)

## Tips

- The transfer case filler plug and drain plug are both hex-socket type (17mm Allen key). Do not confuse with the transmission pan bolts.
- Fill until fluid runs out of the filler hole — this is the correct level. Do not try to estimate volume.
- If the drained fluid contains metallic particles or looks like grey sludge, inspect the transfer case for damage before refilling.
- Always check that the filler plug is removable before draining — if it is seized you cannot refill.
- The transfer case is located behind the transmission and is accessible from underneath with the vehicle on ramps or a lift.
- If you drive in water crossings or off-road mud, change the transfer case fluid every 20,000 km — water contamination degrades the fluid rapidly.

The Pajero IV automatic transmission has an oil cooler integrated into the radiator (a heat exchanger in the lower tank) plus an external cooler on some models. The cooler passages become contaminated with burnt fluid and debris over time. A cooler flush is recommended when replacing the transmission fluid after the transmission has overheated, or when fluid is very dark and smells burnt. It is done in addition to the transmission fluid change, not instead of it.

## Symptoms

- Transmission fluid very dark or smells burnt (overheating history)
- Transmission fluid changed but shifting quality did not improve
- Transmission overheating warning or harsh shifting in hot weather
- Dark sludge visible when draining the transmission pan

## Tools needed

- Drain pan (minimum 10L capacity)
- 10mm socket (transmission pan bolts)
- Transmission cooler line disconnect tool or flat blade
- Hand fluid pump
- Torque wrench

## Tips

- The most practical flush method on a home driveway: disconnect the cooler return line (the line from the cooler back to the transmission), place it in a container, and run the engine for 5–10 seconds at a time, topping up the transmission fluid between short runs. Repeat until clean fluid exits.
- Do not run the engine for more than 10–15 seconds without adding fluid — running the transmission dry damages the pump.
- Identify the cooler return line: it is the lower of the two lines connecting the transmission to the radiator cooler. On the Pajero IV it connects at the transmission side with a push-fit or banjo fitting.
- If the existing fluid is black with a burnt smell, consider dropping the transmission pan first to clean the magnet and inspect for debris before flushing the cooler.
- Cheap ATF degrades faster in a cooler — always use SP-III. If using an aftermarket fluid, confirm it meets Mitsubishi SP-III specification.

The Pajero IV INVECS-III 5-speed automatic transmission has an internal filter (strainer) inside the transmission pan. Mitsubishi does not publish a filter replacement interval — it is treated as part of a fluid change at major overhauls. In practice, replacing the filter with every second or third fluid change (every 80,000–120,000 km) is good preventive maintenance, particularly if the pan drops show metallic debris or dark sludge. The filter is accessed by removing the transmission pan.

## Symptoms

- Routine transmission service or high-mileage preventive maintenance
- Metallic debris on drain plug magnet when dropping the pan
- Sluggish or delayed gear engagement, especially cold
- Transmission shudder during torque converter lock-up (4th/5th gear cruise)

## Tools needed

- 10mm socket (transmission pan bolts)

- Magnet (to collect debris from pan)
- Torque wrench

## Tips

- The transmission drain plug (if present) drains only the pan — drop the pan to access the filter and inspect for debris.
- Inspect the pan magnet carefully: fine grey metallic fuzz is normal bearing and clutch wear; larger particles or flakes indicate internal damage requiring further investigation.
- The strainer is press-fit into the valve body. Prise it off gently — do not use excessive force. Confirm the O-ring comes out with the old filter, not left in the housing.
- Clean the pan with solvent before reinstalling — old sludge reduces the capacity of the new filter immediately.

All Pajero Gen IV models (2008–2013) come with 5-speed automatic transmission. The transmission fluid (ATF) serves as coolant, lubricant, and hydraulic medium for valve actuation. Over 60,000 km, the fluid oxidizes, additives break down, and friction modifiers deteriorate, reducing shift quality and transmission efficiency. Factory service intervals call for fluid changes every 60,000 km or earlier if the vehicle is used for towing or heavy city driving.

## Symptoms

- Scheduled maintenance interval (60,000 km, or 40,000 km if towing)
- Transmission fluid appears dark brown or smells burnt (indicates overheating or oxidation)
- Sluggish or delayed shift response (transmission hesitates 1–2 seconds before engaging gears)
- Shuddering during acceleration (worn friction material in torque converter or fluid breakdown)
- [object Object]
- Leaking red fluid under the vehicle (ATF is bright red when new, darkens with age)

## Tools needed

- Transmission pan drain wrench or socket (usually 17mm hex)
- Pan or drain container with 10+ liters capacity
- Gasket scraper or plastic scraper to remove old pan gasket material without scratching
- Rags and shop towels in large quantity (ATF is messy)
- Hand pump or electric oil pump to refill transmission (much faster than a funnel)
- Transmission fluid dipstick to check final level (already in vehicle)

## Tips

- Perform this service when the transmission is warm (idle the engine for 5 minutes before draining). Warm fluid flows faster and carries more contaminants out
- The transmission pan is mounted under the vehicle, below the transmission. You will need to raise the vehicle on a lift or jack stands to access it safely
- Drain the pan slowly — ATF is hot and can cause burns. Position the drain pan directly below the drain plug
- Let the pan drain for at least 15 minutes. Some residual fluid will remain in the pan and torque converter (maybe 2 liters), so expect the system to need less than full capacity for a complete refill
- [object Object]
- Scrape the pan sealing surface clean of old gasket material. Use a plastic scraper to avoid scratching the aluminum surface
- [object Object]
- Reinstall the pan with a new gasket, tightening bolts in a cross pattern (alternating opposite sides) to ensure even seating
- Refill the transmission with ATF to the "COLD" mark on the dipstick if the transmission is at room temperature, or "HOT" if the transmission is still warm
- After refilling, start the engine and move the shift lever through all gears (P-R-N-D) several times while holding the brake. This circulates the new fluid through the torque converter
- Recheck the dipstick after this procedure. The level may have changed as air bubbles purge from the system
- Drive the vehicle gently for 10–15 km, then recheck the transmission temperature and fluid level. A proper fluid change improves shift quality noticeably

The Pajero IV uses a 5-speed automatic transmission (Aisin) that relies on transmission fluid for lubrication, cooling, and pressure. - Transmission fluid absorbs moisture (hygroscopic). Leaving the cap off or driving through water can introduce water into the fluid, causing corrosion.

- Never use power steering fluid or engine oil in the transmission — it will cause immediate failure.

The V5AWF 5-speed automatic transmission fitted to the Gen 3 and Gen 4 Pajero (both diesel 4M41 and petrol 6G75 variants) uses an overflow-plug fill system — fluid is added until it flows out of a separate overflow hole at the correct level. There is no conventional dipstick. The drain-and-fill procedure replaces approximately 3 litres of fluid per pass (roughly half the total capacity), so multiple passes are needed for a full flush. Always check the fluid level after any fill using the ATF temperature-check procedure to confirm correct fill.

## Symptoms

- Transmission slipping or jerky gear changes (low or degraded fluid)
- Dark, discoloured, or burnt-smelling ATF on the filler plug dipstick
- Gearbox warning light or fault codes related to fluid temperature
- High-mileage service (40,000–60,000 km intervals recommended)

## Tools needed

- Drain tray (minimum 4 L capacity)
- Funnel and extension hose to reach the filler plug
- Socket set — filler, overflow, and drain plugs are typically 24 mm or similar
- Torque wrench
- Rag and protective sheet (fluid will drip onto centre member — place a protective sheet)

## Tips

- A single drain-and-fill only replaces about 50–60% of the total fluid — the rest remains in the torque converter. For a thorough fluid change, do two passes (drain, fill, drive 10 km, drain again, fill).
- The overflow-plug fill system ensures the correct fluid level at the correct temperature — do not overfill by adding extra fluid above the overflow point.
- If fluid did not drip from the overflow hole after refilling, the level is too low. Do not drive — add more fluid until overflow drips occur.
- Keep the protective sheet under the centre member — ATF is a skin and paint irritant and is very slippery on concrete.
- Some Pajero variants use a fluid with a specific colour (red ATF SP-III) — if the drained fluid is brown-black, it has been in service too long.
- The factory recommends ATF SP-III. Some owners use Dexron VI (backwards compatible with III), but verify compatibility with Mitsubishi specifications for your year.

V5AWF Automatic Transmission — ATF Level Check

The V5AWF automatic transmission has no dipstick — fluid level is checked through an overflow-plug system and is critically temperature-dependent. The check must be performed with fluid at exactly 39–46°C. The transmission has a built-in ATF temperature warning lamp (on the instrument cluster) that tells you when you are in the correct temperature window. Checking at the wrong temperature gives a false reading. This procedure explains how to warm the transmission and verify the level without a scan tool.

## Tools needed

- Wire or paperclip to ground OBD diagnosis connector terminal No. 1
- Socket set for overflow and filler plugs
- Torque wrench
- New overflow plug gasket (always replace)
- New filler plug O-ring (always replace)
- ATF to top up if needed

## Tips

- If checking in warm weather (ambient above 20°C), the ATF may reach temperature faster — watch the warning lamp instead of relying solely on time.
- The ATF temperature warning lamp is not a permanent fixture on all Pajero instrument clusters — check your specific year. If the lamp is absent, use a transmission temperature reader (OBD adapter with live data) to verify temperature.
- The N → D → N cycling trick warms the converter faster than just idling — it forces fluid circulation through all hydraulic circuits.
- Always use new sealing washers/O-rings when removing the overflow and filler plugs, even if just checking the level.
- If topping up, use only the specified ATF (SP-III or equivalent). Mixing incompatible fluids can cause clutch pack damage.

V6 Petrol — Hydraulic Lash Adjuster Tick (Lifter Noise)

The V6 engines (6G72 3.0L and 6G74 3.5L) use hydraulic lash adjusters (HLA) instead of manually-adjusted valve clearances. These HLAs are filled with oil under pressure to automatically maintain zero valve clearance. After sitting overnight, oil drains from the adjuster and causes a ticking noise on start-up — this is normal and should clear within 30–60 seconds. Persistent or loud ticking after warm-up indicates air in the adjuster, incorrect oil level, or worn/dirty HLA units.

## Symptoms

- Ticking or clattering from the valve train on cold start, clearing within 30–60 seconds (normal)
- Persistent ticking after the engine is fully warmed up (abnormal)
- Ticking that remains after repeated throttle cycling exercises
- Loud clatter at idle that does not improve

## Tips

- Fresh engine oil is essential — sludged oil cannot flow quickly enough to keep HLAs pressurised.
- If the vehicle has been sitting for months, oil has fully drained from the HLAs and ticking may take several minutes to clear on first start — this is normal.
- Use the correct viscosity oil (typically 10W-30 or 5W-30 per Mitsubishi spec). Thick oil at cold temperatures is slow to prime the HLAs.
- If one HLA is worn or stuck, the tick will be localised to that cylinder. A mechanic with a stethoscope can pinpoint the noisy cylinder.
- Persistent HLA ticking despite fresh oil and air-bleeding usually means the adjuster is worn and needs replacement — this is an engine-out job.
- If the oil looks milky (coolant contamination) or is very dark and smells burnt, change the oil before attempting the air-bleed procedure.
Check OEM part number for your specific model year
Verify the number of teeth — 3.0L and 3.5L differ

The 6G72 (3.0L) and 6G74 (3.5L) V6 petrol engines use a timing belt driving dual overhead camshafts. Belt failure will cause catastrophic valve/piston damage as these are interference engines — a snapped belt at any speed will bend valves. Replacement interval is 90,000–100,000 km or 5 years (whichever comes first). Always replace the water pump, belt tensioner, and idler at the same time as they share the same labour. This is an expert-level job requiring engine disassembly and careful timing mark alignment.

## Symptoms

- Cracking or glazing visible on belt surface (inspect through inspection cover)
- Engine suddenly dies and will not restart (belt snapped)
- Rough running, backfiring, or poor power after extended high-mileage service
- Squealing from the front of the engine (worn tensioner bearing)

## Tools needed

- Full socket set (metric)
- Torque wrench (up to 200 Nm for crankshaft pulley bolt)
- Crankshaft pulley holding tool or strap wrench
- Timing belt tension gauge (preferred) or factory tensioner tool
- Coolant drain tray and hose
- Marker or whiteout pen (for marking TDC and timing reference points)
- Camera / phone to photograph belt routing before removal

## Tips

- Take multiple photos of the belt routing and all timing marks before removal — you will thank yourself during installation.
- Mark the belt rotation direction with an arrow before removal — install the new belt in the same direction.
- The crankshaft pulley bolt is extremely tight. An impact wrench is the safest way to remove it — trying to break it loose with a breaker bar risks breaking the crankshaft position sensor.
- Check the condition of the cam seals and front crankshaft seal while the covers are off — replace them if weeping. Seal kits are cheap; re-doing the timing belt to fix a seal leak is expensive.
- After completing the job, mark the date and odometer on a sticker inside the engine bay.

The water pump circulates coolant through the engine block, radiator, and heater. Most pumps are belt-driven by the serpentine belt and last 80,000–120,000 km before the bearing wears out or the seal fails. A failed pump causes coolant leaks, overheating, and in severe cases, catastrophic engine damage (warped head, cracked block). While the job is moderate difficulty, it requires draining coolant, removing the serpentine belt, and careful reinstallation of gaskets.

## Symptoms

- Coolant leak from the front of the engine (pooling under the radiator)
- Overheating or slow warm-up (pump not circulating coolant efficiently)
- Grinding or squealing noise from the front of the engine (bearing wear in pump shaft)
- White crusty buildup around the pump housing (coolant seeping past the seal)
- Engine coolant temperature gauge slowly creeping up despite normal ambient temperature
- Sweet smell from engine bay (coolant leak)

## Tools needed

- Socket set and wrenches for serpentine belt pulley bolts (typically 10-14mm)
- Gasket scraper or plastic scraper to remove old pump gasket (plastic recommended)
- Serpentine belt tool or reference to check belt condition and routing diagram
- Coolant drain pan with minimum 2 liters capacity
- Torque wrench with 20-100 Nm range for pump bolts and pulley

## Tips

- Before removing the water pump, inspect the serpentine belt. If it is cracked, frayed, or glazed, replace it at the same time. The labor cost is minimal compared to pulling the engine again
- The water pump routing is critical. Take a photo of the belt routing BEFORE removal so you can replicate it exactly. Incorrect routing causes overheating and belt slipping
- Some engines (especially the 6G75 petrol) have the thermostat housing bolted directly to the water pump. If removing the housing, you may disturb the thermostat. Have a fresh O-ring seal on hand
- The water pump bearing can seize if the vehicle is stored in a cold climate and not started for weeks. If the bearing is stuck, do not force it to rotate by hand. Soak the pump in penetrating oil (WD-40, Liquid Wrench) for 15–30 minutes, then try again
- After the first 100 km of driving, recheck the coolant level and inspect the pump area for leaks. The gasket may need additional torque or bedding-in time
- If the engine still overheats after pump replacement, the thermostat or radiator may be the culprit, not the pump. Have the thermostat checked (see thermostat-replacement.yml)

Wheel alignment sets the angles of the tyres relative to the vehicle and road. Correct alignment prevents uneven tyre wear, reduces rolling resistance, and keeps the vehicle tracking straight. The Pajero IV uses an independent front suspension and a live rear axle, so front alignment (toe and camber) is adjustable; rear alignment is largely fixed. Alignment should be checked after any suspension component replacement, kerb strike, or if the vehicle pulls to one side.

## Symptoms

- Vehicle pulling left or right when driving straight on a level road
- Uneven tyre wear — worn on inner or outer edge only
- Steering wheel off-centre when driving straight
- Vibration or wandering at highway speeds
- After suspension component replacement (arms, ball joints, tie rods)
- After a heavy kerb strike or pothole impact

## Tips

- Always check tyre pressures before an alignment — under-inflated tyres change the contact patch and make alignment readings inaccurate.
- Front toe is the most commonly out-of-spec measurement and causes rapid inner or outer tyre wear. Easy to see on worn tyres before alignment is measured.
- Camber on the Pajero IV front suspension is partially adjustable via eccentric bolts on the lower arm. Some workshops skip this and only adjust toe.
- After any steering or suspension repair (tie rod, lower arm, ball joint), always get an alignment — the geometry will have changed.
- Alignment costs $60–120 at most tyre shops. It's the cheapest tyre preservation service you can buy.
- If the vehicle pulls only under braking (not at cruise), the issue is brake balance or a sticking caliper, not alignment.
- Off-road driving on rough terrain gradually knocks alignment out. Check annually if you drive off-road regularly.
Balancing is a tyre shop service (~$15–25 per wheel). Weights are consumables and wear off over time.
If runout is severe, wheel bearing may be loose or damaged
See rear-axle-bearing-inspection or wheel-alignment topics for bearing play diagnosis.

Wheel imbalance (uneven weight distribution) and tire runout (out-of-round or side-to-side wobble) cause vibration and accelerated tire wear. Factory service intervals call for wheel balance checks after tire replacement or if vibration develops. Runout is a tire or wheel defect measured with a dial gauge; balance is corrected by adding weights. DIY diagnosis is visual and tactile; precision measurement requires a wheel balance machine at a tyre shop.

## Symptoms

- Vibration in the steering wheel or floor at highway speeds (70–100 km/h)
- Vibration that decreases at higher speeds or appears only within a narrow speed range
- Uneven tire wear (one edge wearing faster than the opposite edge)
- Tyre wear on one side of the tread pattern (cupping or scalloping)
- Visible wobble or run-out when spinning the wheel by hand (out-of-round)
- Steering wheel shimmy on acceleration or braking

## Tools needed

- Dial indicator or runout gauge (tyre shop equipment)
- Wheel balancer machine (tyre shop equipment)
- Jack and jack stands

## Tips

- Imbalance vs runout: imbalance causes up-and-down vibration, runout causes wobble. Imbalance is corrected by adding weights; runout is corrected by truing the tire or replacing the wheel.
- Visual check for runout: spin the wheel by hand (with the vehicle jacked up) and watch the edge of the tire. It should remain perfectly round with no side-to-side wobble. Any visible wobble indicates runout.
- Vibration at one specific speed (e.g., exactly at 80 km/h) is often imbalance — the resonant frequency of the car matches a harmonic of the wheel rotation. Vibration across a range of speeds is more likely runout or a bearing problem.
- After tire replacement, always have wheels balanced. Even a new tire can have manufacturing imbalance. Balancing adds $15–25 per wheel to a tire job.
- Stick-on weights (adhesive) are lighter and look neater on alloy wheels. Clip-on weights work on steel wheels and are reusable. The choice depends on your wheel type.
- Runout tolerance: typically ±3 mm radial (vertical wobble) and ±2 mm lateral (side-to-side). Wheels exceeding this should be re-trued or replaced.
- If vibration persists after balancing, suspect driveshaft runout, engine imbalance, or suspension wear — not a wheel problem.

Power window faults on the Pajero IV are usually a blown fuse, a failed window switch, a worn motor, or a broken regulator cable. The driver's door master switch controls all windows and has a window lock button that disables the passenger switches — check this first. Individual window failures point to the motor or switch for that door; all-window failures point to the main fuse.

## Symptoms

- One window not responding to switch (motor or switch fault)
- All windows stopped working (fuse or master switch fault)
- Window moves slowly or stops midway (worn motor or jammed regulator)
- Window works from master switch but not from door switch (door switch fault)
- Grinding or clicking noise when operating window
- Window drops into door and won't rise (regulator cable snapped)

## Tips

- Check the window lock button on the driver's master switch — pressing it disables all passenger window switches. This is mistaken for a fault regularly.
- Check fuse 19 (15A) in the interior fuse box for the power window circuit. A blown fuse stops all windows.
- To test if the motor is the fault: apply 12V directly to the motor connector. If it runs, the switch or wiring is at fault. If it does not run, the motor is dead.
- Window regulator cables fray and snap on older vehicles, particularly in humid climates. The window will drop freely into the door when the cable breaks.
- Window switch contacts corrode on doors that are frequently exposed to water ingress. Cleaning the switch contacts with contact cleaner often restores function.
- If a window works only intermittently and gets stuck when hot, the motor brushes are worn — it works when cold but fails when the motor heats up.

The Pajero IV uses hook-type wiper arms. Replace blades when they streak, chatter, or leave unwiped bands — typically every 12–18 months or sooner in harsh climates. The front uses two different-length blades; the rear uses a shorter blade with a different attachment. Always replace front blades as a pair — a new driver blade against an old passenger blade causes uneven clearing.

## Symptoms

- Streaking or smearing across the windscreen during wiping
- Chattering or juddering as the blade crosses the glass
- Unwiped arc or band left on the windscreen
- Blade sitting away from glass at one end (split or hardened rubber)
- Squealing wiper noise at low speed

## Tools needed

- No tools required — blade clips by hand

## Tips

- Lift the arm fully away from the glass before removing the blade — if it springs back without a blade fitted it can crack the windscreen.
- Lay a folded cloth on the bonnet under the arm before lifting, as insurance if it drops.
- The release tab is on the underside of the blade where it joins the arm hook. Press it toward the glass and slide the blade down and off.
- Clean the windscreen with glass cleaner and the wiper arm hook with a rag before fitting the new blade — a gritty hook scratches the new blade insert immediately.
- If new blades still streak, the windscreen may have a silicone or wax film from car wash products. Clean with an isopropyl alcohol wipe before wiping down with the new blade.
- Rear wiper blades on the Pajero IV use the same hook fitting as the front — confirm the arm style before ordering, as some late-model vehicles use a pinch-tab adapter.

The Pajero IV wiper system is controlled by the combination switch on the steering column and a wiper relay in the engine bay fuse box. The most common faults are a blown fuse, a failed wiper relay, a seized wiper motor, or a failed wiper link assembly. Intermittent failure often points to the relay or a loose earth connection. Complete failure on all speeds usually means the fuse or relay, not the motor.

## Symptoms

- Wipers do not operate on any speed (check fuse and relay first)
- Wipers operate at HI and LO but not on intermittent — combination switch fault
- Wipers do not park at the rest position and stop mid-sweep — park switch in motor
- Rear wiper not working but front works — separate fuse and motor
- Washer pump not working — separate pump fuse, pump blocked or failed
- Rain sensor wipers not responding — sensor adaptation required after windscreen replacement

## Tools needed

- Multimeter (voltage and continuity)
- Fuse puller

## Tips

- Check the wiper fuse first — it is in the engine bay fuse box, labelled WIPER or FRONT WIPER. The rear wiper is on a separate fuse labelled REAR WIPER.
- To test the relay: pull it out and swap it with an identical relay from the same fuse box (horn relay or similar). If wipers now work, the relay is the fault.
- If the wipers stop in the middle of the windscreen: the park switch inside the motor is faulty. The wipers should always return to the parked position even after the stalk is switched off.
- Rear wiper failure: check the rubber-sealed connector in the tailgate hinge area first. This connector flexes every time the tailgate opens and the contacts corrode. Clean with electrical contact cleaner.
- Washer pump not spraying: check that the washer bottle is full. Then disconnect the hose at the pump and blow through it — a blocked jet nozzle causes apparent pump failure.
- If wipers work on LO/HI but not INT: the intermittent wiper function is part of the combination switch. Replace the switch rather than diagnosing the internal contacts.
- Rain sensor (auto wiper) adaptation: after windscreen replacement, the rain sensor must be adapted to the new glass using a scan tool or by performing the adaptation procedure in the factory manual.

East Coast Loop: Egilsstaðir to Höfn
A scenic drive along Iceland's east coast, through fishing villages, beneath waterfalls, and past the Vatnajökull glacier.

The east coast of Iceland is often overlooked by tourists rushing toward the Ring Road. It's worth the detour. Fewer cars, better roads, and a rugged beauty that feels less curated than the south coast.

## Route 1 from Egilsstaðir

Starting from Egilsstaðir, Route 1 hugs the coastline. The road is excellent—wide, well-maintained, free of the gravel and washboard that characterizes the north. We pushed south toward Höfn at a steady pace.

## Waterfall Country

Fjaðrármúli, Skaftafell, Svartifoss—each valley seems to have its own waterfall. We stopped frequently to explore. The Pajero's turning radius and low ground clearance made it easier to park near trailheads than larger SUVs. One morning, we hiked to Svartifoss through hiking-boot country; the Pajero sat in the parking area, a reminder that some places are meant to be reached on foot.

## Vatnajökull & the South

As we approached Höfn, Vatnajökull dominated the horizon. The glacier's ice tongues—Breiðamerkurjökull, Skaftafellsjökull—descend into the valleys like frozen rivers. We took the glacier tour on foot; the Pajero watched from the car park.

**Fuel consumption:** 8.2 L/100km on Highway 1. The engine runs efficiently at steady cruising speeds.

**Next stop:** We're aiming for Húsavík by week's end. Pushing north through the interior again.
A journey into Iceland's interior, navigating F-roads through snow and exploring geothermal areas around Geysir and Haukadalur.

The Langjökull highlands in early spring are unforgiving. Fresh snow on the plateau, roads barely passable, but the Pajero handled it with grace. We pushed north from Reykjavík toward the interior, following Route 1 and then veering onto the F-roads that cut through the heart of Iceland.

## Day 1: Route 1 to the Interior

The drive from Reykjavík took longer than expected—snow conditions deteriorated as we climbed elevation. The Pajero's all-terrain tires found purchase where lesser vehicles would struggle. We made camp near Deildartunguhver, where hot springs steam against the arctic backdrop.

## Day 2: Langjökull Plateau

F39 was challenging but rewarding. The plateau stretches endlessly, broken only by geothermal vents and the occasional fox. We navigated by landscape more than GPS. The weather cleared for a few hours—long enough to see the Langjökull glacier from a distance and understand the scale of what we were crossing.

## Day 3: Descent & Geysir

On the way back, we detoured to Haukadalur Valley. Geysir itself was quiet, but the geothermal field around it—Strokkur erupting every few minutes, the smell of sulfur, the sound of boiling water beneath the earth—reminded me why this country demands respect.

The Pajero's suspension handled every washboard section and river crossing. At 248,310 km on the odometer, it's still proving itself.

**Gear:** Frontiermen camp stove, MSR Dromedary bags for water, extra fuel (no stations on F39), sat communicator.

**Rig status:** All systems nominal. Added skid plate protection before the trip. No issues.
North Highlands: The Road Less Travelled
Winter travel in the north—F-roads closed, but secondary routes offer solitude and a reminder of why the Pajero exists.

February in North Iceland is harsh. Most F-roads are locked behind snow gates. But the alternative routes—unpaved but drivable—open up a different kind of adventure. Fewer tourists. More silence.

## Akureyri to the Interior

We left Akureyri early, fuel tanks full, extra food and a satellite communicator. Route 1 was dry until we turned inland toward Blönduós. From there, Route 68 climbs into the highlands.

The road deteriorated gradually—asphalt to gravel to washboard to talcum-powder dust. The Pajero's suspension absorbed it all. At this point in the trip, the drivetrain feels tighter than when we started 17,000 km ago.

## The Highlands at Winter

There's a peculiar beauty to empty landscapes. No cars. No tourists. Just the sound of wind and the occasional ptarmigan. We camped in a sheltered valley near Tjörnes, where geothermal heat keeps a small area ice-free even in winter.

## Mechanical Notes

The 4M40 diesel runs smoothly at altitude, where petrol engines gasp. We used extra fuel heating the cab at night—roughly 2L per night with the cabin heater running moderately. Manageable.

**Tire performance:** The Bridgestones are performing well even at 248,310 km. Tread depth is borderline for another winter, though. Planning a tire service in Akureyri before the next expedition.

**What's next:** Heading south toward Reykjavík for scheduled maintenance and resupply. Route back to Húsavík to pick up some expedition members.
South Coast Classic: Vik to Jökulsárlón
The most photographed stretch of Iceland in winter—black sand beaches, glaciers, and a highway that tests every season.

Everyone drives Route 1's south coast. In summer, it's crowded with rental cars. In winter, it's a gauntlet of ice, wind, and beauty that separates the committed from the casual.

## Vík & Reynisfjara

We started at Vík, where the black sand beach and basalt columns meet a violent Atlantic. Wind gusted to 60+ km/h. The Pajero sat steady on its suspension while lighter vehicles rocked in the gusts.

Reynisfjara beach is iconic—for good reason. Waves crash over the basalt, seabirds circle, and the landscape feels alien. Winter visits fewer tourists, which means better light and fewer crowds. We had the beach almost to ourselves.

## Skaftafell & the Glaciers

Pushing east on Route 1, we entered glacier country. Skaftafellsjökull descended into its valley, pristine and vast. The tour operators were running their last trips before winter closures; we declined and explored the hiking trails on our own.

A full day from Vík to Jökulsárlón took 5 hours with photo stops. Route 1 is excellent here—no surprises, good visibility, manageable.

## Jökulsárlón

The glacier lagoon in winter is surreal. Fewer tours, calmer conditions, and a particular quality of light—low angle in February, turning the ice blue and gold. We watched a calving event: a small iceberg the size of a house broke from the glacier and splashed into the lagoon.

**Fuel:** 7.8 L/100km on the highway, 8.2 L/100km accounting for exploration driving. Managed the tank well between Vik and Höfn.

**Route assessment:** South coast in winter is safe if you drive carefully. The Pajero's all-terrain tires gave confidence even on ice-glazed sections.
